column11: The District’s inclusionary zoning law “requires that eight to 10 percent of the residential floor area be set-aside for affordable units in most new residential development projects of 10 or more units.” The cost of doing so is paid by a project’s developer, and IZ units are income-restricted. Because the number of IZ units is tied to floor-area ratio, the larger a building is, the more IZ units will be built. Conversely, if the initial density proposed by a developer is reduced during the approvals process, rather than maintained or increased, fewer IZ units will be built. ANC commissioners are likely to hear from some constituents concerned by a project’s potential impact—real or assumed—on traffic, parking, views, and property values and rents, and whether it fits the character of the neighborhood. While it is not a guarantee that a development proposal including IZ units will come before your ANC, if one does, what would you do, given the likelihood of at least some pushback?
column12: Planned unit developments are projects in which developers are able to exceed the height and density allowed by the District’s zoning code up to the density allowed by its Future Land Use Map (typically a modest increase in scale) in exchange for a community benefits agreement. column27: The Sustainable D.C. 2.0 plan includes a target of reducing commuter trips made by car to 25 percent. Do you agree that incentivizing residents and visitors to drive less should be an explicit policy goal of the District?
column28: Incentives for people to drive less and nudges to make them drive safely can prevent drivers from killing other people, but research shows such “carrots” won’t be enough to curb the public health crisis of increasing traffic fatalities. Keeping people alive and safe on District roads requires policies that actively reshape our transportation systems and built environment to decrease single-occupancy vehicle trips, and to slow down drivers when they do make those trips. Please rank the following policies in the order that you would like to see the District government pursue them.
column39: The above question asks about systemic policies to reduce trips by car that the District’s executive and legislative branches would need to initiate. column50: The biggest issue in Woodley Park is the combined lack of dense housing and a road design that doesn’t support safe travel for cars, bicycles, buses, or pedestrians. Woodley Park has great potential for increased housing density along its commercial corridor on Connecticut Avenue. To address the District-wide housing shortage, we should build new housing and increase density where feasible throughout the District. Additionally, to support new residents, we must ensure Connecticut Avenue is redesigned for safe, multi-modal travel and that the commercial corridor is upzoned to allow for increased height, more housing, and vibrant public spaces.  I will promote the redevelopment of Connecticut Avenue based on the community-endorsed Concept C. I will advocate for upzoning Connecticut Avenue's commercial area in Woodley Park to allow building heights of up to 90 feet, in line with the recommendations from the Historic Preservation Review Board. Additionally, I will collaborate with all stakeholders to remove regulatory barriers that currently prevent a grocery store from occupying the vacant commercial space in Woodley Park.
column51: In my opinion, the role of an ANC Commissioner is one of sole public utility. Commissioners ought to serve as a responsible, honest, and effective advocate for the interests of the entire community.     Commissioners have a unique opportunity to engage with constituents from a diverse range of convictions on local issues. Pragmatic public servants are able to find the common ground across misconceptions of irreparable difference, build a coalition that respects their neighbors, and advance opportunities that equitably serve their constituents and the District at-large.
column52: I have strong convictions towards the effective utility of local government and have strong experience in engaging with diverse voices on local issues. column8: Allowable density should be increased everywhere in 5D. Throughout 5D, community leaders are working to reduce crime, make the streets safe, and improve student   outcomes. If these efforts are successful, higher-income households will gain further interest in moving here. Since our housing stock is limited by zoning, this will mean that the new households may muscle out long-term renters by spiking rents and sale prices for existing homes even more than already has happened.    Because of this, we should permit ample new homes of all kinds in and around these neighborhoods to absorb these new residents while also creating new housing opportunities for existing residents and their families. This way, we can slow and hopefully reverse displacement, just as has happened in Downtown Silver Spring (one of the only areas in the DC region to  experience "inclusive growth" - growth of high-income population without loss of low-income population).    While the question does not ask this, I believe that the upcoming Comprehensive Plan should also prioritize additional density in Ward 3, especially near the Metrorail stops. Constructing much more housing in these affluent communities would absorb a lot of the demand currently coming to 5D communities such as Trinidad, and would thus slow or reverse rent increases.

column8: I believe Georgia Avenue, which stretches across ANC 1E, is a great place to increase density and build a strong, more affordable community. This includes making investments in three keys areas: housing, public transportation, and businesses. Georgia Avenue has a number of under utilized lots and properties that can support housing developments without displacing current residents. I have been a vocal advocate for increasing affordable housing opportunities that attract low and middle income families, including testifying before the DC Council’s Business and Economic Development Committee in support of Bruce Monroe. In addition to housing, Georgia Avenue and adjacent streets have many public transportation opportunities with plans to increase options and safety in the works. This is an important component to expanding density so residents can access jobs, schools, and other amenities without relying on cars. Finally, increased density promotes a vibrant retail environment. Boosting foot traffic and establishing a consistent consumer base supports and expands opportunities for our local business. New developments need to include greater retail spaces and work with the community (such as District Bridges’ Lower Georgia Av Main Streets) and the city (such as the Deputy Mayor for Planning and Economic Development) to recruit and support a diverse set of local, small businesses, not just restaurants and bars.

column51: I believe that in order to successfully tackle an issue, residents need to be at the center of any solution. My resident-centered approach provides four steps for working with the community to understand an issue and develop solutions that work. 1) Assess: We start the process by researching the situation to understand the problem and its full impact on the community (community forums, surveys, engaging SMEs, etc.). 2) Develop: Armed with knowledge, we draft a comprehensive package outlining the issue, presenting the evidence, and providing recommendations. The package will act as the community’s voice to the appropriate District agencies and other stakeholders. 3) Advocate: We draft and execute an action plan around delivering and promoting the community’s comprehensive package to appropriate District agencies and other stakeholders. 4) Evaluate: We set benchmarks for success and continuously monitor activities to ensure progress is happening. Additionally, we evaluate the impact of our solutions once they are implemented. If necessary, we work together to conduct additional assessments and adjust/amend the package. This approach promotes collaboration and transparency for the community.
column52: Over the past two years, I have delivered results on critical issues important to residents. Part of my success has been consistently applying my resident-centered approach. For example, we developed the “8th Street Transportation Package” that started with an on-the-ground assessment of four major concern areas identified by more than 110 residents (street lights, traffic, parking, and sidewalks). Next, we researched policies and guidelines to draft 10 recommendations that address the issues in each of the four areas. The community was asked to review, prioritize, and add to the recommendations using a public survey, which was completed by more than 80 individuals. The final package included 13 recommendations that ANC 1E unanimously approved. Since passing the package, we have successfully implemented and secured more than half of the recommendations. During my second term, I will continue to increase opportunities for residents to have meaningful input and direct access to the ANC and District agencies. I will promote the communities voice and work to ensure the District continues to serve residents.

Specifically, areas like 14th Street NW and Georgia Avenue NW are prime candidates for increased density. These corridors already have a mix of commercial uses and could be ideal locations to grow residential use as well. By focusing on these areas, we can create more housing options while also supporting local businesses and enhancing walkability.    It's important to ensure that any new development is thoughtfully integrated into the existing community, expanding our neighborhood in a way that welcomes residents living in all types of housing—affordable, mixed-use, renters, and homeowners alike. By increasing density, we can help address the city's housing needs while also contributing to the vibrancy and sustainability of our community.

column52: I believe I am the right person to continue serving as an ANC commissioner for my SMD because of my deep commitment, passion, and extensive experience in this role. Over the past nine years, I've worked tirelessly for our community, both as a volunteer and as a Commissioner, driven by my love for our neighborhood, community, and neighbors, and my great pride in the District of Columbia.    In addition to my work as Commissioner, I've been actively involved in initiatives that have made a tangible difference in our community. I co-founded Friends of 16th Street Heights and played a key role in transforming Hamilton Park, creating vibrant spaces. I also co-founded the Uptown Farmers Market, and Uptown Main Street, which has contributed to the revitalization and support of our local businesses.    My ability to think creatively and develop out-of-the-box solutions allows me to address challenges effectively, while my strategic mindset ensures that I can see the bigger picture and implement long-term strategies for the benefit of our community. Additionally, I am a master relationship builder and generator, which has been crucial in forming strong connections with DC agencies, elected officials, residents, local businesses, and other stakeholders.    With my proven track record, passion for service, and strategic approach, I am confident that I am the right person to continue serving and making a positive impact as your ANC commissioner.

column52: Over the past five years, I have volunteered on the ANC Alcohol Licensing Committee and have since expanded my service to include the Economic Development and Zoning/Preservation/Development Committees. In May, I was sworn to fill the vacancy as the ANC1B-02 SMD commissioner.    With my growing understanding of the Duke Plan (Office of Planning's Small Area Plan) and the Mid-City Arts Overlay zoning, I am committed to fostering planned discussions with my ANC colleagues and government leaders that focus on balancing the residential and commercial needs within our community. Perhaps a "comeback plan" for U Street—similar to the current initiatives focused on Penn Quarter/Chinatown and Downtown commercial to residential conversion areas—could be introduced into our grassroots government lexicon.    I support action which help establish a BID/CID place management entity to address a range of deficiencies, such as insufficient trash collection and cleaning, the absence of ride-sharing drop-off and pick-up stations, and the need for enhanced public safety measures. Additionally, it is imperative to recalibrate city focus on leveraging 32 other By-Right land uses within the Arts Overlay—beyond the “eating/drinking” establishments, which are just one of the 33 prescribed uses—to boost 18-hour pedestrian traffic, thereby improving public safety.    In summary, I am dedicated to the review of and action on measures which support the quality of life and community enhancements for the approximately 15,000 North Shaw/U Street residents who are lucky to call this area home. This effort includes our Black-owned businesses and the preservation of the area's cultural history, both of which will benefit from renewed attention to economic growth and its very related public safety initiative.

column8: Allowable density should be increased everywhere in 5D. Throughout 5D, community leaders are working to reduce crime, make the streets safe, and improve student outcomes. If these efforts are successful, higher-income households will gain further interest in moving here. Since our housing stock is limited by zoning, this will mean that the new households may muscle out long-term renters by spiking rents and sale prices for existing homes even more than already has happened.    Because of this, we should permit ample new homes - both market-rate and income-restricted - in and around these neighborhoods to absorb these new residents while also creating new housing opportunities for existing residents and their families. This way, we can slow and hopefully reverse displacement, just as has happened in Downtown Silver Spring (one of the only areas in the DC region to experience "inclusive growth" - growth of high-income population without loss of low-income population).    While the question does not ask this, I believe that the upcoming Comprehensive Plan should also prioritize additional density in Ward 3, especially near the Metrorail stops. Constructing much more housing in these affluent communities would absorb a lot of the demand currently coming to 5D communities such as Trinidad, and would thus slow or reverse rent increases.

column52: TL;DR: I am a consistent supporter of local businesses, pedestrian safety, outdoor dining, and new housing. I've consistently supported permits for the new bars, restaurants, and streeteries that have come into the neighborhood, and as Zoning Committee chair for 5D, I have worked closely with affected ANCs and developers to build consensus on housing proposals to put forward to the whole ANC. column50: Right now the biggest issue in our neighborhood (ANC) is the redevelopment of the civic core. The city has committed to this project, to include funding, and the RFP deadline is fast approaching. As with most development projects, this project so both neighbors in support, opposition, and those that wanted to shape the project to maximize community interest. As we approach the RFP selection process, I plan on continuing to listen and advocate for the redevelopment of the library and community center. We have young families in dire need of a new, modernized space for their kids to learn, play, and grow. Our senior neighbors deserve a space where they can enjoy recreation programming in a modernized environment. I plan to work hard to balance the need for affordable housing in an area of the city that has limited supply, with the community's call for retaining green space as much as possible.  Continue to work with the community on maximizing their participation during the RFP process to select a development team that will be a good partner in the development process, who is also willing to work to address community concerns in an effort to maximize housing opportunities. I will continue to support the Mayor's goal of providing affordable housing opportunities at the civic core site.
column51: In my role as an elected representative, I have always approached decision-making with a deep sense of responsibility and dedication to serving my constituents. I believe in the power of listening to the voices of those I represent, seeking guidance from subject matter experts, and carefully considering all feedback received.     I make it a priority to connect with my community in various ways, from knocking on doors and sending out informative emails to producing a comprehensive ANC-wide newsletter. Engaging directly with constituents on the issues that impact our neighborhood and the ANC as a whole is not just a duty but a passion of mine.    I go above and beyond by hosting meetings with District officials, organizing exclusive Zoom sessions for my SMD, and participating in local block parties to establish personal connections with the people I serve. In today's climate of negativity, division, and unfair treatment of ANC Commissioners, I believe that maintaining open and transparent lines of communication with constituents is more crucial than ever.    With significant matters such as the civic core and the Maret Field Project unfolding in ANC 3/4G, I recognize the importance of actively listening to concerns, gaining a deep understanding of the issues at hand, and providing constituents with the opportunity to engage with me directly. By fostering meaningful dialogue and ensuring that voices are heard, I strive to represent the best interests of my community with integrity and empathy.
column52: I firmly believe that I am the ideal candidate to represent SMD 3/4G-01 due to my unwavering commitment to public service and the well-being of my community. My decision to run for ANC Commissioner was driven by a genuine desire to serve as a responsible steward of our neighborhood.    My candidacy was motivated by a vision to enhance infrastructure, improve public transit, promote walkability, ensure public safety, and foster strong connections with local schools, particularly Lafayette Elementary School. I sought to support our senior neighbors, engage with young families, and address the diverse needs of our community.    My continued dedication to running for this position is rooted in a genuine desire to serve and uplift our community, not out of spite, protest, or a desire to undermine the institution of the ANC. I am committed to ensuring that every voice in our community is included and heard, especially when crucial decisions are being made that directly impact their lives. Our community is divided on a lot of issues and we need Commissioners that are true public servants, that can find common ground and good. My goal is to serve as a passionate advocate for the people of SMD 3/4G-01 and work tirelessly to make our community an even better place for all who call it home.

column50: Housing affordability. Washington, D.C. is among the most expensive cities in the country, and it’s becoming increasingly difficult for residents to find housing they can afford without the fear of being priced out. Everyone should have the opportunity to live and own property in the District without needing multiple high incomes in the household. I view this issue through a public health lens, recognizing that housing is a fundamental need and a key social determinant of health. Stable and affordable housing is critical for long-term health outcomes and is closely linked to broader issues such as crime, safety, education, and mental health. With this understanding, I fully support the development of diverse housing options not only in my SMD and ANC but across the entire District.  Actively advocate for the development of varied housing types (apartments and condos with varied unit sizes, not only studios; row houses that can house single families) that meet the needs of all residents. I would support projects that maximize housing density within legal limits and promote the establishment of essential businesses and services, such as libraries, parks, and medical facilities, to create a well-rounded, accessible community. I would also support and promote non-essential businesses that increase the quality of life for residents, such as restaurants, coffee shops, and other types of stores.

However, I recognize that not everyone has the time or opportunity to provide input on every issue. As an ANC commissioner, I will focus on addressing the fundamental needs and dignities of my constituents, including housing, public safety, accessibility, and access to essential services like food, healthcare, and jobs. My decisions will be guided by the goal of enhancing the well-being of our community while also considering the broader interests of the District as a whole.

column8: I gave a comprehensive answer to this question last year and got a ribbing for it in my endorsement. There isn't any developed land in my Commission Area I wouldn't support increasing density on.    That said, the highest ROI prospect for Commission pressure/intervention at the moment would be NewCityDC (aka Montana Triangle), the 15-acre site currently controlled by Douglas Development at the junction of New York Ave NE, Montana Ave NE, and Bladensburg Rd NE. In 2006, this collection of lots was slated for 3,400-3,600 homes and 148,121 SF commercial (per artifacts in ZC 06-15). More recently, Douglas planned for 2,380 homes, 338,464 SF retail, 177,889 office (per their promotional materials). An active zoning case (ZC 24-11) seeks to amend the map for much of the land back to industrial uses without offering many details or community offerings that might set it up for successful residential near-term, and the next ANC 5C will likely have a huge role to play in how that plays out.

column8: Density is important for new housing in the East of the River Shopping Center in NE DC for several reasons:    	1.	Efficient Land Use: Increasing the density of housing allows for more efficient use of available land, which is especially important in urban areas where land is limited and often expensive. By building more units in a given area, the shopping center can accommodate more residents without needing to expand the footprint of the development.  	2.	Supporting Local Businesses: Higher residential density brings more people into the area, which in turn supports local businesses. More residents living near the East of the River Shopping Center will likely shop, dine, and use services in the vicinity, helping to boost the local economy and creating a more vibrant community.  	3.	Improved Public Transit Access: Higher density housing often justifies and attracts better public transportation options. In NE DC, this could mean more frequent bus service, potential Metro expansions, or other transit improvements, making it easier for residents to commute and reducing reliance on cars, which can decrease traffic congestion and pollution.  	4.	Affordable Housing Opportunities: By increasing density, developers can include a mix of housing types, including affordable units, which is crucial in areas experiencing gentrification or rising housing costs. This helps ensure that a diverse range of people, including those with lower incomes, can afford to live in the community.  	5.	Environmental Sustainability: Higher density housing typically has a lower environmental impact per capita compared to lower-density suburban developments. Concentrating housing in a smaller area reduces the need for extensive infrastructure (like roads and utilities) and preserves more green space, leading to more sustainable urban growth.  	6.	Community and Social Cohesion: Dense housing can foster a greater sense of community, as people live closer together, interact more frequently, and can easily access shared amenities. This can lead to stronger neighborhood ties and a more cohesive community atmosphere.    Overall, increasing density in the East of the River Shopping Center’s new housing development can lead to a more vibrant, sustainable, and inclusive community in NE DC.

column51: 1.	Community Engagement: Actively engage with constituents through meetings, surveys, and social media to understand their concerns and needs. Although it’s impossible to hear from everyone, making efforts to reach a diverse range of voices ensures more comprehensive representation.  	2.	Data-Driven Decisions: Use data and research to inform decisions. This includes understanding the demographics, needs, and trends within the community and the district.  	3.	Transparency and Accountability: Be transparent about the decision-making process, providing clear explanations for decisions and being open to feedback. This helps build trust and ensures accountability.  	4.	Balancing Local and District-Wide Interests: While prioritizing the interests of your specific district, also consider the broader implications for the entire city. This means supporting initiatives that benefit the district as a whole, even if they may not directly impact your area.  	5.	Collaboration: Work with other commissioners, local organizations, and government agencies to find solutions that address multiple perspectives and needs.  	6.	Ethical Considerations: Always act with integrity and in accordance with ethical guidelines, prioritizing the common good over personal or political interests.    By combining these approaches, an ANC commissioner can make informed, fair, and balanced decisions that reflect the interests of both their constituents and the entire District of Columbia.

column52: It is with great honor that I am able to present my candidacy for the seat of ANC Commissioner in the SMD 6/8F02. I believe that I am the right person to serve as an ANC commissioner because I understand the urgent matters that are being presented right now. As a District of Columbia native, born and raised in Ward 8, and now homeowner in this great city — it is my goal to find sensible solutions to address the concerns of my community. I aim to address the urgent matters that may arise in the future planning of our beloved community. As a mom to an amazing son, I am honored to share this journey in the place that we call home. As a product of the District of Columbia Public School System, where I graduated at the top of my class, it has been an honor to pay it forward to the future generations by serving on the DCPS Parent Advisory Committee for Out of School programs. As a current District of Columbia homeowner, I know firsthand what it means to fight for the longevity of housing, a continuous topic dear to my heart.     Growing up, I have seen firsthand the growing challenges that our community continues to face, as well as the resources that are continuously unavailable. As an avid community advocate throughout our city, I have always stepped up to fight for better education, safer streets, and stronger community bonds in the place that we all call home. I have a proven record of already having done the work and building the relationships needed to get the job done. I truly understand the issues and levels of work needed to do the job at hand. I believe in doing the hard work. That is the exact reason why I feel that the time is right to continue the fight that I started in my youth to now.     I will fight for community improvements and make strides to strengthen our community by building collaborative efforts to push forward for the common good. I am confident that I have what is needed to make our neighborhood the best place that it can possibly be.

column50: The issues that seems to have galvanized the neighborhood the most in the past year have been crime and personal safety, controversy over bike lanes along Columbia Road, and rat control.  Keep solutions that have worked and try new ideas for the areas where we haven't been successful. Neighbors came together to explore various ways to combat the crime such as hiring a private security guard, creating neighborhood watches, increasing communication of crime in real time via What's App, and the creation of a crime task force.  I would continue to support these initiatives and facilitate solutions created by residents.  The issue of bike lanes is ongoing in the neighborhood.  There has been a huge lack of communication regarding the planning and implementation of the Columbia Rd bike lanes. Given that more bike lanes are in the planning, a big first step in my SMD is to communicate to the residents what is happening and explain how their voices and opinions can be heard.  A lot of residents were blindsided by the current plans and had no idea what was happening. Ideally residents know what is happening in their neighborhood and know where to express approval or disapproval. If elected, I hope to increase awareness of the initiatives taking place to alter the infrastructure, public transportation, and crime enforcement in this SMD.

column52: Running for ANC representative for ANC 1C04 feels like the natural next step for me. I’ve called the Adams Morgan neighborhood home for over 25 years, watching DC evolve—sometimes for the better, sometimes not. I raised my son here as a single parent, and over the years, my connection to this community has only deepened. As I see him and the students I’ve taught over my 30 years as a teacher in the District return here to build their future, I am all the motivated to put my energies into making and keeping DC a fantastic place to live and raise a family. I love DC and I love my neighborhood!    I’m a firm believer that democracy works best when everyone’s voice is heard. But as I’ve participated in community surveys and discussions, I’ve noticed a troubling trend: too many questions present binary choices, as if complex issues can be boiled down to yes or no. This kind of thinking is not only simplistic but can also lead to decisions that don’t reflect the reality of our diverse community. Issues like bike lanes, bus routes, green spaces, and crime rates aren’t black-and-white; they require thoughtful consideration of all perspectives. We need to move away from either/or thinking and embrace a both/and mindset that seeks compromise and balance.  That’s why I’m running—I’m committed to finding common ground, especially when the issues are complicated. How do we make sure our plans work for the senior citizen, the veteran in a wheelchair, the single parent juggling three kids, the Spanish-speaking immigrant, the young professional fresh out of college, and the newly married couple just starting out? We need solutions that work for everyone, and that takes creativity and flexibility.    Transportation and infrastructure decisions in Ward 1—and throughout DC—should reflect the needs of the entire community, not just the loudest voices or  the newest residents. I want to be the voice for long-time DC residents, advocating for smart growth and sensible transportation plans that prioritize buses over bikes and sidewalks over scooters when it makes sense. I’m a big supporter of the 15-minute city concept, where essential services and amenities are within a short walk or bike ride for everyone. This vision is especially important for long-time homeowners like myself, who want to stay in our homes for as long as possible.     Who is this city for? Who is the neighborhood for? What makes a livable, successful community? How can we ensure that the senior citizen who has trouble walking or the differently abled can get from their home to the grocery store without fearing being knocked down by a distracted cyclist or young adult on a scooter? How can we ensure that a mom or dad with 3 children can park their car close to their house and can get all three kids to soccer practice on time when they are the carpool driver and the practice fields are scattered all over the DMV?  How can all residents feel safe walking home from the bars at night when the crime rates vary wildly across the different wards? How can citizens regain confidence in the police department’s ability to arrest and convict wrongdoers when the crime lab had such a long period when it was not accredited? How can we ensure that public schools and public transportation continue getting the funding necessary to thrive? How can we increase the density of the city, but preserve the character of neighborhoods with Victorian row houses or grand Tudor homes with huge yards? These are just a few of the questions we need to tackle together.    I can’t claim to have all the answers - - in fact, I have very few answers. I am not an expert with all the answers. Rather, I see my role as someone who can bring people together, facilitate conversations, work toward consensus, and keep folks informed about what’s happening in the neighborhood. I’m running for ANC Commissioner to find the best solutions for all of us. That’s why I believe I’m the right person for this position—because I’m focused on lifting up as many voices as possible in the hopes that that our community grows in a way that benefits us all.

Specifically, I would advocate for increasing density in areas that have the potential to support higher-density housing due to their proximity to public transit, retail, and other amenities. column50: The most pressing issue in our neighborhood is public safety, particularly the ongoing violence. This problem is compounded by the lack of opportunities and safe spaces for children to simply be children. Our community’s youth and adults also face significant challenges due to inadequate access to the resources and support they need to succeed. I believe that addressing these interconnected issues is crucial for the well-being of our community. We must create safer environments, provide more opportunities for our children to engage in positive activities, and offer better support systems for both youth and adults. This holistic approach is necessary to break the cycle of violence and pave the way for a brighter future for all residents.  Given the commission’s limited authority, I would focus on advocating for increased funding and resources for community centers, after-school programs, and job training initiatives. I would also work closely with local law enforcement and community organizations to implement strategies that enhance public safety, such as neighborhood watch programs and increased visibility of police patrols. Additionally, I would foster partnerships with local businesses and nonprofits to create mentorship and internship opportunities for our youth, helping them build skills and confidence for their future.  By taking these steps, I aim to make our neighborhood a safer, more supportive place where every resident, from children to adults, has the opportunity to thrive.
column51: As an ANC commissioner, I recognize the importance of representing the approximately 2,000 constituents in my SMD while also upholding the broader interests of the District of Columbia. I understand that while I may not hear from every single constituent during my term, it is my duty to consider the needs and perspectives of all residents when making decisions.  To ensure I act in the best interest of both my SMD and the entire District my decision-making as an ANC commissioner would be guided by a commitment to active engagement, data-driven analysis, collaboration, and a balanced approach that considers both the needs of my constituents and the interests of the entire District.

column8: Every neighborhood in D.C. shares an equal responsibility for increasing housing density and making affordable and deeply affordable housing available to our residents. It is not enough to only flip single family homes, potentially displacing long-term residents, to build a couple million-dollar units. We must also build affordable and multi-family housing that is actually financially accessible to average residents and also build deeply affordable housing. I am committed to making housing justice a reality in our ANC and would support higher density projects, especially those that drive foot traffic to Upshur Main Street, which needs additional business to thrive. I would specifically suggest housing on the small stretch of 9th street above the local businesses and the D.C. Superior Court Drop-In Center with the goal of driving foot traffic to Upshur Main Street. I support building more density on Georgia, New Hampshire, and all legally permissible major thoroughfares.

column50: My SMD borders Upshur Main Street, which needs additional foot traffic and business to thrive. Last year, I bartended at a local restaurant on Upshur and felt the direct effect of losing that additional income - which was more acutely felt by the full time staff - when the business went under. Staff tried to keep the business afloat through local events - including a series I started for fellow LGBTQ+ neighbors called “Out on Upshur” - but it wasn’t enough given the exorbitant rent and challenging business conditions. Many neighbors have expressed concern about the high turnover of local businesses and lack of support for local entrepreneurs, including many of the long standing businesses. My own father owned a small kitchen and bath contracting business when I was growing up, so I’m especially familiar with challenges faced by small business owners. I want to be clear that my interest in local business development is not the only concern I see within the community. I am equally invested in increasing housing density, reducing gun violence, creating more bike lanes, making roads safer for bikes and pedestrians, making public transportation better, continuing to find ways to make Grant Circle safer, increasing rat abatement and alley cleanups, increasing tree cover and beautification projects, and improving communication between officials and residents.  Connect with the local business owners, business association, landlords, workers, and other Commissioners to solve chronic problems.  I would also find new ways to communicate with residents to make sure they can identify their ANC, what we do, and what issues they face.  Also, I would promote the creation of more community events and spaces for youth to spend time.  I would make myself a resource to the community, organize alley clean-ups, be in touch with city agencies about rat abatement, and work on the Commission’s housing priorities, bike safety, and Grant Circle and intersection safety.
column51: I’m a public servant working on policy issues in the federal government, so I understand how to make nonpartisan decisions for the benefit of a wide variety of people. The diversity of our neighborhood is what makes it great, so I’m interested in meeting with anyone who has input on decisions that impact the neighborhood.  I’m also a long time community organizer in D.C. and know how to build coalitions, manage community funds, execute on a strategic vision, and treat everyone fairly and respectfully. I want to make sure I am reaching our aging neighbors, long-term residents, and new residents that may require different outreach methods.
column52: My first introduction to a Commissioner was in 2017 when someone was tragically shot in front of my house on the corner of Varnum and Kansas in our ANC. Our Commissioner appeared on the scene to make sure we were okay and to liaise with the police when members of my house were unwilling or uncomfortable doing so. I hope to be similarly visible as a Commissioner and member of the community that shows up for neighbors in whatever ways they need. This requires finding effective ways to communicate with neighbors and understanding what is going on in the neighborhood in addition to tackling all of the more classic ANC tasks related to Commission work.    In addition to my experience as a public servant, I’ve been a long time community organizer in D.C. and also worked at a local business in my ANC. I also know the challenges of being a renter and homeowner because I’ve rented in three different houses in our ANC and now live with my sister, who owns our home. Our housemate runs a mobile bike repair business in the neighborhood which also raises my awareness of various issues for bikers in our ANC. As a community organizer in D.C., I’ve learned about macro and micro concerns within the community. My experience organizing has ranged from prison abolition issues, including reforms at D.C. Jail and participating in Jail Support, housing justice organizing, and LGBTQ+ organizing including bringing the Dyke March back to DC. I’d be silly not to plug an appearance I made in a GGWash article on the displacement of long term D.C. residents: https://ggwash.org/view/72081/dc-dykes-march-against-displacement-lgbtq-housing-washington

column8: In my ANC, increased density to increase new housing is needed, particularly along the critical Georgia Avenue corridor.  There is currently underway, the much-needed Park Morton-Bruce Monroe housing development project, however, more can and should be done to increase density, resolve the dearth in affordable housing and increase economic development along Georgia Avenue. Outside of the Park Morton-Bruce Monroe development projects, there a number of vacant properties along Georgia Avenue and transportation infrastructure that provide ripe opportunity for affordable housing and mixed-use development, both of which are important for the viability of the corridor and access to housing for lower-income DC residents. Efforts should leverage the work of important stakeholders, like Howard University, that are planning projects to increase density, and must involve substantive community engagement with other key stakeholders like community members, including Park Morton residents, the ANC commissioners, developers, service providers, our council members, and the civic organizations that are vibrantly active in this ANC. Community engagement is vital to ensure that construction reflects housing development that sustainably meets the needs of all residents.

column50: A long-term priority for our neighborhood is the revitalization of Georgia Avenue, specifically the development of the Park Morton and Bruce Monroe affordable housing project. Our neighborhood has watched this project move in fits-and-starts since the early 2000s, and the community is eager to see the project move forward. The Park Morton-Bruce Monroe project is central for increasing density on Georgia Avenue and moving the economic development of the corridor forward. The project is even more critical given the devastating effects of COVID on small businesses on Georgia Avenue.  If elected, I would continue to support affordable housing, ensure that the Park Morton community benefits package is implemented in a way that reflects the interests of the Park Morton residents, and the broader community. I would also keep community members informed of the project’s progress and facilitate engagement among stakeholders. I will also track Georgia Avenue bus lane projects which are intended to address safety concerns.
column51: I believe strongly in the power of community engagement and communication. As someone who is deeply involved in my community, I find these are valuable tools. In making decisions, if elected, I would stay informed of residents’ concerns by listening to them and addressing issues through connecting with the full range of stakeholders, including constituents, other ANC commissioners, council members, District agency and law enforcement officials, private sector actors (developers, property, and business owners) faith-based leaders, and the strong network of civic organizations active in our community. My ANC is socially, economically and politically rich in diversity. To be an effective commissioner, it is essential to create and build relationships, so that any decision I make as an elected commissioner, is informed by diverse perspectives, backed by evidence-based information, and importantly, reflects community participation in the decision-making process through giving citizens voice and ensuring their interests are represented. People want to be heard. If elected, I would look forward to attending civic association meetings, reaching out to District officials and representatives for information or support, or linking constituents with officials so they can engage with officials themselves to find solutions. All of this, in my view, enhances citizen agency, and government responsiveness and accountability, and closes the gap between constituents and officials.
column52: I have been an active resident of Park View for over 20 years, and it has been my pleasure and privilege to engage with my neighbors and community as president of the Luray-Warder Neighborhood Association, a founding member of Georgia Avenue Thrive, member of the Lower Georgia Avenue Main Streets Neighborhood Council, and of the Park Morton Steering Committee. I’ve had my fingers on the pulse of what’s important to our community and I have long been committed to being a positive agent of change and to keeping Park View moving forward and accomplishing great things as a community.      I am a co-founder of Georgia Avenue Thrive, an all-volunteer DC neighborhood community revitalization initiative focused on public safety, activating vacant property, promoting and recruiting small businesses, building community cohesion and placemaking activities. I mobilized Park View volunteers and businesses to hold movie nights on Georgia Avenue at the old Murry’s market making use of vacant spaces, community clean-ups, and also helped organize the first and very successful Fall Fest at Bruce Monroe Park bringing the community together on Georgia Ave.    As a member of the Park Morton Steering Committee, representing members of the Luray-Warder Neighborhood Association throughout south Park View, I engaged with neighbors on the contents of the community benefits package, testified before the DC Zoning Commission in support of the project and PUD, and kept community members informed of the project’s progress and engaged them in planning and design activities. On the Lower Georgia Avenue Main Streets Neighborhood Council, in support of economic revitalization of Georgia Avenue, I work with local businesses and stakeholders to promote growth of our small businesses along Georgia Ave through providing small grants, technical assistance, enhancing local foot traffic, and building relationships in the community.     These experiences are inspiring. I want to translate all of my experiences into representing my SMD on ANC1E02 as an elected commissioner.  I am excited about the prospect of working hard with community members to address issues that are of important to residents through creating awareness, encouraging community input and participation, and making sure residents are heard in decision-making if I am elected. I have established productive relationships with community members, local officials, law enforcement, businesses, and other civic leaders. If elected, I look forward to the opportunity to leverage those relationships to accomplish goals of affordable housing, enhancing public safety and service provision, and promoting clean and beautified spaces in not just for my SMD, but all of Park View.

column8: All of it, and not just ANC 1B but all of Ward 1. Not only is DC in a housing crisis, we are in a climate crisis and a traffic safety crisis, and for all of these reasons, places well-served by transit like the U Street corridor, the Georgia Ave corridor, and the 16th Street corridor must make more space for more neighbors.    First of all, we need to create more homes for the people who are here, and then we need to make *more* homes for the people who are coming. DC and the region continue to grow, and will continue to grow--and if we want to do anything about our high and rising prices, we need to *make more homes* for those people.    But beyond that, we are in a climate crisis. Our world is burning---DC has set records for heat this summer, and, as a recent US DOT report states, "America cannot meet its [climate change] goals...without reducing how much Americans drive" <https://usa.streetsblog.org/2024/07/29/feds-warns-congress-that-americans-need-to-drive-less-to-survive-climate-change>. It is imperative that we make more housing, and make housing more affordable, in places where people can take the bus, ride a bike, and walk for most commuting, errands, and exercise.    Further, we are in a road safety crisis. 2023 saw the most traffic fatalities in DC since 2007, and as August dawns we are on pace to meet or exceed last year's total <https://mpdc.dc.gov/node/208742>. We must make our roads safer, and again part of doing that is giving people the ability to live where they don't need to drive.

column49: I already minimize my driving within the city—I commute, socialize, and run nearly all errands on transit, bike, or foot. Nearly all of my current car use is to go with my partner to visit our families, who live in places that are not (yet) accessible by Amtrak within a reasonable span of time or at a reasonable hour.    I fully support the District's goals to reduce trips by car, and work professionally to help communities across the Mid-Atlantic become easier to get around on foot and by bike. I look forward to working as a Commissioner to make it easier for neighbors in ANC 1B and across DC to reduce their car use, both by supporting more transit, biking, and walking infrastructure, and by supporting bringing more housing, jobs, groceries and so forth within walking and biking distance of each other.
column50: I feel the biggest issues in my neighborhood are housing costs, traffic violence, and climate change, which, as I have already described, are interlinked.      But I think the biggest issue not already addressed is the seeming inability of DC Government agencies to function under this administration. DDOT is unable to make repairs to sidewalks and bikeways in a timely manner, and can't or won't enforce or provide Safe Accommodations at construction sites, including its own. DPW doesn't have the resources to ticket or tow scofflaw drivers, but also doesn't focus its resources on those who do the most harn, spending its staff time instead on easy targets like overstayed meters and people without residential parking permits. DPW also would rather remove "abused" public trash cans than empty them frequently enough (or investigate why residents, particularly of multifamily buildings, put their trash in them rather than their home cans and dumpsters). And OUC, particularly 9-1-1, is melting down.  I look forward to working with fellow Commissioners and our colleagues across the Ward and across the District to push agencies to be more responsive to our constituents, and to working with our Councilmembers to hold agency leaders responsible, find the systemic issues causing these problems, and create solutions and the oversight to ensure they are implemented, both during Performance Oversight/Budget in the spring and throughout the year.
column51: Like most Commissioners, I already have one full-time job, and I intend to invite people to email me, and will consider having a periodic 'office-hours' for people to come meet me in person and putting out newsletters, but even those efforts will never reach every single constituent.    I will make decisions based on what I believe is best for the District and its residents, particularly what is most likely to reduce traffic violence, housing prices and homelessness, and the impacts of climate change, and to improve transit service, walking, and biking and create increased housing and tree canopy.
column52: I am a transportation planner, and as mentioned above work across the region to improve public spaces and places for people walking and biking. I'm also a member of DC's Pedestrian Advisory Council, and was recently enthusiastically reappointed to my second three-year term as Ward 1 Representative. I've testified at Council both on behalf of the PAC and for myself, primarily but not only on transportation issues.    I've held board positions both in professional organizations (DC chapters of Young Professionals in Transportation, Association of Pedestrian and Bicycle Professionals) and community organizations (Reed-Cooke Neighborhood Association, Better Streets Lawrenceville [Pittsburgh]), have organized DC's edition of the Ride of Silence memorial bike ride for victims of traffic violence since 2019, and was recently appointed as a Member at Large of the board of Ex Lapide, Virginia Tech's Society for queer alumni and allies.    I am deeply curious and collaborative, so I have both knowledge of and connections throughout the District and its agencies, and look forward to working with my colleagues on the Commission, at Council, and across the District, rather than against them.

column8: Density can and should be increased throughout ANC 1B. During the last almost 4 years (2 terms) as Advisory Neighborhood Commissioner, I have been an active proponent of increasing housing and overall density throughout the Commission. For example, a PUD came up for my individual SMD (1250 U St NW) which will exponentially increase the housing supply (increased height and density) in ANC 1B, as no housing currently exists on the plot. This development also adds even more housing by developing an alley lot behind the larger building.    Also, during my time, two public parcels came up — The Reeves Center and 1617 U St NW.     The Reeves Center went through the public disposition process and RFP. Currently, there isn't any housing that exists here, and one of the main priorities from the community was to increase that. As a Commissioner (This was in my SMD prior to redistricting), I helped advocate and push for more affordable housing here, especially deeply affordable housing, maximizing what could be built since it is a public parcel.    1617 U St NW/1620 V St (the FEMS and 3D MPD HQ public parcel) is still in the early stages; however, for the majority of my second term, I was a strong advocate for the map amendment/upzoning for the parcel from MU-4 to MU-10. This map amendment will increase the density and height of the future development, allowing for multiple uses for the site, including the return of FEMS, 3D MPD HQ, and housing. Although the footprint of the MU-10 was reduced for a compromise with opponents, I will still make sure the community's priority for more housing, especially affordable housing, will be added here as the public disposition and RFP process progresses.    In ANC 1B, there are still other opportunities to build even more housing. First, there is another public parcel that may come up for development at S St NW. This will hopefully follow the processes above (although if there is a map amendment or zoning change, it hopefully won't take over a year to come to fruition). It is currently zoned as RF-1, and since it is a public parcel, it may be worthwhile for it to be upzoned.    ANC 1B is diverse in the types of buildings and zones, with even a fair amount of public parcels in comparison to other ANCs. I believe another area of opportunity to increase housing would be in residential zones, increasing ADUs and adding units onto the tops of homes when possible. Although this wouldn't dramatically increase housing like in the public parcels described above, it would still add more housing to the supply, especially in the vibrant area of ANC 1B.

column50: Safety is the biggest issue in my neighborhood. While some may limit its definition to crime and violence, that is an oversight. Community members feeling and being safe is a more holistic and interconnected matter. Safety revolves around having access to housing, job opportunities, reliable public transit options to get around, and access to resources like schools, health services, and more. Safety is more than just crime prevention, and the influential matters that come before the ANC can help to improve safety.  If elected, I would continue what I'm doing and help advocate for the community's overall well-being. Whether it's small items like ensuring a public trash can is emptied or urging developers to build more housing, I understand my role as an ANC and believe that I can continue making a positive impact in my community.
column51: I do my best to be an active member in my SMD and community overall. I attend walks from various agencies, town halls, meetings, and events from organizations. I am also out every day walking my own neighborhood with my dog. I believe my heightened physical visibility allows me to see what is happening firsthand and gives me the opportunity to meet and listen to my neighbors too.  At the same time, I try to stay abreast of what is happening across DC, meet with other ANC Commissioners, and look at what other jurisdictions outside of DC are doing to improve, so I can apply those insights to ANC 1B.
column52: ANC 1B is a very busy Commission with multiple housing projects, public parcels, many liquor licenses, and transportation developments (11th St protected bike lane, 14th St Bus Priority Project, and more). Coming up in 2025, ANC 1B may have over 50% first-time serving ANC Commissioners. With that, I believe ANC 1B and Ward 1 continue to need an experienced, thoughtful, and strong Commissioner, like myself, to be re-elected. I am nearing the completion of my second term as an ANC Commissioner and have served as Chairperson for ANC 1B for almost 2 years now. I am experienced and confident that with my determined persistence to work through challenges, prior efforts engaging with the community, and drive to get the job done, I am the best person to represent 1B08.

column8: I think the greatest opportunity for increasing housing density in Anacostia is along the waterfront. Outside the boundaries of Historic Anacostia, this area can accommodate taller, higher-density development. It provides access to mass transit along the Green Line, the bus routes, and the Beltway, without adding to increasing congestion on MLK Blvd. and other main arteries in Anacostia. This can also create more connections between Anacostia Park and the community (currently separated by the 295), giving current residents access to more recreation and amenities, bringing increased foot traffic and economic vitality to local businesses, creating local jobs, and expanding Anacostia’s tax base to support our schools and services.    Another big opportunity is addressing the vacant houses in the neighborhood. Given the housing shortage, there are far too many houses sitting empty. We need to incentivize owners to fix them up and get people moved in. And this can create more opportunities for local home ownership, so people don’t have to move out to move up.

column49: Two types of trips account for most of my car travel: grocery shopping and visiting parks with my dog. My biggest wish for Anacostia is that we get a full-service grocery store within walking distance of most neighbors. Another top wish is to have better access to Anacostia Park and better activation of the park. Right now, to enter the park you have to go either through the Marion Barry Ave. underpass (and one of the most hazardous intersections in the city) or take a long walk down Howard Ave. We need an easy-access bridge that opens it up to everyone—seniors, youngsters, and everyone in between. And when you enter the park, it’s still pretty car-centric. It needs to make better use of the space for strollers, picnickers, fishermen, athletes, runners, bicyclists, AND dog owners.    I don’t mean to dodge the question of which car trip substitution I can commit to now; I want to underscore that the city has to invest in the infrastructure to create a more walkable, connected community.
column50: We need a full service grocery store in Anacostia; I want to help make it happen.  I would work with the community, DC government, and developers to plan for a full-service grocery store in the heart of Anacostia. This means hearing what the community wants in terms of amenities and location; working with the city agencies like DMPED and DDOT to ensure that incentives and infrastructural planning will support not just building a grocery store, but its long-term service to the community as we grow; and negotiating with every current and future developer to gain their investment in this tentpole of a thriving community.
column51: My campaign will be focused on asking neighbors two questions: What do you love about the neighborhood, and what would you like to change? If elected, their answers would guide my initial efforts. I would hold regular SMD meetings to hear from neighbors, share current and upcoming issues before the Commission, and get their feedback. I would also participate with the existing neighborhood organizations, like the Historic Anacostia Block Association and the Anacostia Homeowners and Residents Association, who already engage their neighbors. And I would continue having conversations by walking the neighborhood. (No one wants more Zoom meetings.)
column52: I love my neighbors and my neighborhood. This is my eighth year here and it’s the friendliest place I’ve ever lived. When people ask what it’s like in Anacostia, I say it’s like living in a small town next to a big city. I hope it stays that way.    But there’s a lot of fast change happening. I want to help protect what’s special about this neighborhood while making sure we get the most from these investments. And I want to help find win-win-wins—listening to people, figuring out what everyone wants, and finding solutions that benefit us all. I think that’s always possible.

column50: My perspective is that SMD 5B05 is a low-density residential neighborhood with streets and avenues that promote highway-style driving (my main concerns are Michigan Avenue, Taylor Street, 12th and 10th Streets). Pedestrians and cyclists of all ages and abilities are often in great peril when sharing the road with drivers going well above the lawful speed limit and/or who do not make full, careful stops as required by law whether at pedestrian crossings or at traffic lights. At the same time, we have a metro station within walking distance and at least seven bus lines with frequent service, plus plenty of cyclists on the road who deserve safe travel lanes. There is more that can be done to improve road safety for all and to promote the use of other modes of transportation. More housing close to public transportation hubs is needed too. Finally, more can be done to upkeep Turkey Thicket Recreation Center, the true anchor and third space of SMD 5B05, for the enjoyment of all residents.  Support 1) bringing necessary changes to ensure the safety of all road users, with an emphasis on the most vulnerable ones; 2) affordable housing close to public transit hubs; 3) local businesses along 12th St. commercial corridor.

column51: Research shows us that neighbors who make public comments at local government meetings are socioeconomically advantaged in a variety of ways. They are more likely to be homeowners, older, and white. In order to properly represent a vibrant and diverse neighborhood like Petworth, I will act to help all my constituents, not just those who have the time to show up to a meeting.   Sometimes, benefits are spread across all of DC, but the costs hit hard in a specific area. Take building a new school, for example. More classrooms and teachers help reduce overcrowding, which is good for everyone in the District. But the construction noise and extra traffic mostly affect the neighbors. If you live next to the proposed school and don't have kids, opposing it makes sense. But if everyone across DC did the same, no schools would get built, and the entire District would suffer. ANCs should consider these District-wide benefits, not just the local drawbacks.
column52: My wife is sick of hearing me talk about zoning cases and would like me to find a more productive outlet for my interest. I'm deeply committed to making our community more affordable. I live in Petworth, care deeply about what happens here, and am ready to advocate for policies that benefit all of us—not just a select few. column8: In my Advisory Neighborhood Commission, I believe that increasing housing density is both feasible and necessary, particularly along 12th Street NE, Rhode Island Avenue, and near the Brookland metro station. Housing is a fundamental human right, and if we approach density increases thoughtfully, we can create more affordable housing while fostering a vibrant community that benefits everyone in the neighborhood and city.    When increasing housing density, it's crucial to align new developments with the neighborhood's needs. For instance, are we focusing on housing for students and young professionals, or are we aiming to accommodate young families? Additionally, it's important to enhance infrastructure to support increased density. This includes adding bike lanes, expanding bus routes, and ensuring access to essential services like grocery stores, schools, daycare centers, and public recreational areas.    Every neighborhood has its unique character, and Brookland is no exception. We must hold developers accountable for creating affordable housing that complements Brookland's distinctive charm by actively engaging with residents and incorporating their feedback, rather than resorting to generic, cookie-cutter designs.

column49: I frequently commute to Adams Morgan, and I am committed to biking there, thanks to the protected bike lanes along the route, particularly on Irving Street. To encourage residents to reduce car usage, it's essential to establish reliable alternatives.    1. Safer Biking: Biking needs to be safe and convenient. The quickest routes often involve major roads, such as Rhode Island Avenue for trips downtown or South Dakota Avenue for travel along the DC-MD border. However, these roads can be intimidating for cyclists. Implementing road diets can help reduce speeding, traffic, and improve safety for all.    2. Reliable Public Transit: While having bike-friendly roads is important, not everyone can bike, and weather conditions can be a deterrent. Therefore, it's crucial for the city to ensure bus routes are well-planned and that buses run on time. Currently, buses can "magically" appear and disappear from tracking apps. Furthermore, using the same example above, traveling from Brookland to Adams Morgan takes 16 minutes by car and 22 minutes by bike, but 43 minutes by public transit.
column50: Safe streets for all: Pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers all deserve to feel safe on the streets. Currently, too many pedestrians risk their safety crossing the street at crosswalks; too many cyclists are afraid to commute by bike; and too many cars are getting into unnecessary crashes.  1. Listen to Neighbors: Gather input from residents to understand their concerns and suggestions regarding street safety.  2. Seek Evidence (trust but verify): Identify areas where redesigns are necessary by reviewing data and analyzing traffic patterns.  3. Collaborate with Commissioners: Work together with other commissioners to translate community feedback into actionable plans.  4. Engage with the City Council and Agencies: Partner with the city council and relevant agencies to implement positive changes in road safety and ensure these initiatives are effectively carried out.
column51: 1. Gathering Public Opinion: To ensure that I reach as many constituents as possible, I will utilize a variety of communication methods. column50: In my SMD, I most frequently encounter issues relating to Meridian Hill/Malcolm X Park, including upkeep and shared use of the space. My general position is that more stakeholders need to actively engage in the management of the park, including District bodies, community groups, local businesses, and philanthropic organizations, alongside (of course) the National Park Service. In my ANC area, the biggest issue certainly is public safety. My position has been that more community engagement and interagency cooperation by law enforcement are urgently required to effectively deter crime and restore public confidence.  Regarding the park, I will continue my efforts to build a constituency of advocates and to engage a broader range of stakeholders to advance the community's clear priorities for maintenance and renovation. As for public safety, I look forward to continuing to lay the groundwork for a routinized community watch/safety walk that involves not only MPD but also the U.S. Park Police, two agencies that have not historically collaborated closely but whose jurisdiction overlaps in my neighborhood. I organized the first-ever safety walk to involve both MPD and USPP officers and look forward to fostering further cooperation in that vein.
column51: At its core, making decisions as an elected representative involves having one's ear to many rails at once. My constituents come first, and I solicit their views on major issues through my e-mail newsletter, for example. At the same time, I am cognizant that the subscribers to my newsletter are not representative of the entire neighborhood, demographically and socio-economically speaking, so I make sure to engage through multiple means, including in-person availability out in the community. I also rely on the expertise and experience of my fellow commissioners to understand the historical context of the actions that the body has taken previously and my responsibility to build upon (and sometimes to correct) that legacy. Finally, I have a talented group of advisors who are professionals in key areas (housing, transit, zoning, and so on), whom I consult regularly for their expert opinions when I am not personally well versed in a policy matter.
column52: I have built strong and mutually productive relationships in my first term, especially those relating to the park and to public safety, and my ability to leverage them to make progress on outstanding issues gives me a head start going into my second term. I have also been elected secretary of my commission and, outside of my ANC work, I am an advocate for democracy reform; these roles give me a unique structural perspective on the commission and how its operations can be improved. Having witnessed some ways in which our internal procedures don't work particularly well, I am committed to redesigning those procedures with a view to transparency and inclusivity. Our commission needs to engage with an ever broader range of voices, and we sometimes fall short on that score. I am qualified and ready to work with my fellow commissioners and the commission staff to implement reforms and open the commission's work to our diverse community of constituents and stakeholders.

column50: Residential street use and parking  The Columbia Road Bus Priority project that is underway, will significantly improve bus and bike travel on a 1-mile stretch of Columbia Road in the heart of Adams Morgan. The dedicated bus lanes in addition to protect bikes will reduce the number of on-street parking options along Columbia Road between Ontario Road and 16th Street. The bus project will also bring several improvements to our community, including better bus service, less traffic congestion, and public space. These changes will take time for residents and visitors to SMD 1C06 to adjust to and become familiar with. As I have during my current term, I will continue to meet with and work the DDOT to find solutions to provide accessible pick-up and drop-off options for residents in large apartment building along Columbia Road that do not have drive-ways. I will also work to provide construction updates and educate my SMD about the changes so that residents can take advantage of faster bus services, access more biking options, and benefit from safer streets.
column51: It's in my nature as a trained sociologist, to gather information. I rely on past meeting notes and videos as well as email exchanges with community members to gather information as well as to get a sense of what the sticking points are. I'm working more regular communication with residents via newsletters and open office hours at local coffee shops, etc. I feel that I have learned about about the basic functions of an ANC during my current term and look forward to building on that experience.
column52: First, I’m a fierce advocate for my immediate neighborhood. I’m constantly in awe of the variety of housing options in my community from row homes, condos, co-ops, group houses, and apartments. Many of our streets enjoy the benefits of a healthy and thriving tree canopy, and so many amenities are within a five minute walk. That said, the area is not without its challenges. We face serious racial and economic equity issues related to gentrification, zoning, and displacement. The business corridors along Columbia Road and 18th Street struggle to keep tenants for a variety of reasons, and pedestrian safety is still a crucial issue in my neighborhood as well as across the District. No one should die crossing the street.    Second, I’m a good listener and I’m not afraid to ask questions. My academic training is as a sociologist, and I work in the federal statistical system with large household demographic surveys. That means that I’m trained to hear and examine multiple sides of a debate using qualitative and quantitative data to inform my conversations with elected leaders and my community.     Lastly, I want to build on what I have learned and achieved in my first term as an ANC. I have a better idea of where I can be effective and where to dedicate my limited time as a full-time worker. If re-elected I would like to focus more on effective rodent control from providing more composting options in apartment heavy Adams Morgan and working with other ANCs. One idea I have is to start an ANC Rat Task Force to help connect residents with rodent control services, as well as advocate for ways to reduce the food waste in our trash system.   Being an ANC is a natural extension of my desire to get more involved and help provide data and my sociological perspective to the conversation about the future of my SMD, my ANC, and Washington, D.C.

I believe that this requires us to be creative and find ways to support increased density both by supporting the construction and preservation of units along commercial corridors, like Mount Pleasant Street, and supporting a range of single and multi-family housing on our residential streets so that families can grow, young people can live, and seniors can age in place without being priced out of Mount Pleasant.    Mount Pleasant is diverse across race and income, and our housing should provide options for everyone.

column50: Overcrowding in our local public schools, especially supporting the creation of the Bancroft Elementary annex.  I think it is essential that we continue to keep the pressure on the city to solve the overcrowding problem. As a former teacher at a Title I public school, I know how overcrowding affects everyone in our school community. It makes it hard for students and families to get the support they need and gets in the way of teachers being able to focus on what matters most - creating a high quality learning environment.
column51: I think it essential for elected officials at every level of government to go out and get to know members of their community, to actively seek out feedback, to create new spaces for community building, and to talk to neighbors who don't necessarily look like them or speak English. I want to invest my time in building relationships in the community, from fostering connections with local nonprofits like La Clínica del Pueblo and The Mount Pleasant Village to introducing myself to local business owners, to making a point of knocking on the door of every neighbor in my SMD and bringing back high quality newsletter connections that so many neighbors remember from the days of a previous ANC commissioner. Beyond this, I deeply believe that Mount Pleasant is a neighborhood for everyone, that no matter your race, income, or what language you speak, that you should be able to live, work, and thrive here. These are the values that will inform all of my advocacy on the ANC.
column52: I know how important it is that a neighborhood work hard to truly provide a welcoming home for everyone. As a former teacher, that means prioritizing the Bancroft Annex so that all families can access high quality public education in their neighborhood. As a young person and renter in the neighborhood, that means supporting the creation of affordable housing and helping city officials identify and tear down barriers to make our housing and affordability plans a reality. As someone who cares about my neighbors, I hear concerns about safety. I think we can take a more holistic approach to safety by better meeting the basic needs of residents and holding city agencies responsible for their end of that bargain.    Mount Pleasant neighbors are a force to be reckoned with when we come together. I'm proud to live here and want to help build this community of people and I think we need the voices of younger people and newer neighbors on our ANC to ensure it represents all of us.

column8: In the current 6A Commission Area, key areas for increasing density should include Benning Road, H Street and Florida Avenue. The AutoZone property at 1207 H Street NE will be the next big project in our ANC and will require careful management and negotiation with developers and neighbors to include as much affordable housing as possible. Our Commission unanimously supported a zoning map amendment that would allow for increased height in exchange for an increased number of affordable units at this location. All of these corridors are directly located on transit lines and could support many more neighbors.     We can also work with neighbors to add Accessory Dwelling Units, housing that can be used to help residents to age in place, create opportunities for multi-generational families to live together, or serve as rental/income generation opportunities. We need to continue to think differently and creatively about how we can increase density and reduce displacement.    I am supportive of growing our housing stock, particularly along transit-rich corridors, as a way to reduce the cost of housing, increase access to housing for teachers, police officers, and other essential workers, and reduce traffic and single-occupancy commuting. Housing, transit, job  opportunities, education, and climate are all interrelated. Improve one and you are making progress on improving all of the others.
