column49: I am fortunate to live in a very walkable and transit-rich neighborhood and conduct nearly all of my errands without a car. When I do go into the office my commute is by bicycle--using the bike lanes on East Capitol and Pennsylvania Avenue to get to my building near Metro Center. When the weather is really bad, I take the metro or ride the D6.    Around the neighborhood, I use my e-bike to haul groceries and my kids to soccer practice whenever I can.  One route I nearly always took by car that I have switched to doing by metro more are my trips to the airport. Getting to Dulles is easy by metro-- and is faster during rush hour. I've taken the silver line for several international flights and it has been great!
column50: Our sense of humanity and ability to care for one another seem to be deteriorating. Everyone has the right to feel safe as they navigate our neighborhood--no matter how they get around.  If re-elected, I will continue to collaborate with DC agencies and community organizations to support comprehensive approaches to strengthen community ties, ensuring all neighbors have opportunities to thrive and feel safe in our community. We need to rethink our  public spaces to be more human-centered and human- scaled as a way to encourage neighbors and community  members to reconnect with one another.
column51: If re-elected I will continue to work with my fellow Commissioners to improve community and traffic safety, collaborate with developers to expand affordable housing, and ensure that restaurant and bar owners act as good neighbors with respect to trash, operating hours  and noise. As I’ve learned over the past seven years, to be a good Commissioner requires listening to all voices—including those who aren’t in the room. I’ve worked hard to reach out to neighbors via my email list, texting, and flyers--and as I walk through the neighborhood each  day. I hope I can continue to serve my neighbors and community through this role.
column52: I am the right person to serve in this role because I have the required passion, persistence and knowledge of how DC agencies work. During my seven years of service I have authored dozens of recommendations to DC agencies—each one informed by evidence, experience, and input from neighbors. On several occasions, this has put me at odds with a vocal minority who don’t want change.  And in every case the end result has been appreciated by the larger community—including many initial opponents. From the beginning of my service I have been transparent about my commitment to safe streets and sidewalks for everyone—no matter how they get around.     My decision to serve followed a near-fatal crash in which a child was hit in the crosswalk by a speeding driver in front of Maury ES. My requests for traffic calming and speed humps at the location of the crash were repeatedly rejected—until I re-analyzed the data, found the analysis used the wrong speed limit and pushed the agency to comply with their own recommendations. I’m committed to continuing to work on public safety, including traffic safety.

column8: Housing affordability is an issue across the district– including in ANC1D. As prices have risen, we’ve lost so many neighbors for cheaper rates in more affordable communities. There are a few keys sites in Mount Pleasant and Northern Columbia Heights that have broad-based community support for additional housing. First, 3215 Mount Pleasant Street, which we all know as the old laundromat. Sadly, the laundromat has closed and the building is for sale. This building could increase in height to accommodate affordable residential units above a new business, which would compliment our vibrant commercial district. Second, the vacant lots on Meridian St NW, commonly known as the Zion Church Lots. These lots are owned by the Church, which wants to sell them to build affordable housing. However, a portion of one vacant lot is owned by District government, which has complicated the sale. One of the first things I did in my first ANC term was to meet with the pastor and community members to determine what they needed to make this sale successful. In April of 2023, I passed a resolution urging District government to move forward with the sale. Unfortunately, the sale is still stalled. In a second term, I will continue to push these issues forward and be a strong advocate for increasing density in a way that works for our community.

column49: With the warming temperatures, I’ve enjoyed riding my electric moped to work about once a week. It’s a short trip and easy by metro, but I’ve so enjoyed driving down Rock Creek Parkway early in the morning and avoiding the business of a metro commute. While it’s enjoyable, there inevitably comes a place along the way where I am not only sitting in traffic, but certainly contributing to it. I can definitely reduce these trips to further the District’s goals. I also recognize that policy fixes, like congestion pricing, would immediately make these trips less desirable for many of us and look forward to supporting more comprehensive systematic solutions if elected for a second term.
column50: In my first term as an ANC Commissioner, I feel really fortunate to have heard from so many neighbors all across my single member district and the commission area. What I hear most frequently is that we’re dealing with dysfunction of very basic services. Our elementary school is overcrowded, there’s litter on every street, the bus is always late, and when we call 911 no one answers. It took over a year to get the District to reinstall public litter cans they randomly removed. If residents can’t trust our government to do the most basic government functions, why would anyone trust them to solve bigger issues?  If elected, I will continue to advocate for our very basic needs, like education, trash, and safety on our streets and sidewalks. In this first term, I’ve learned how to navigate District agencies and built relationships. Now, I want to continue that work and see some critical changes through to completion. One thing I would like to do in a second term is start a transportation-focused committee made up of community members. This committee could advise the ANC on how to prioritize myriad transportation needs on streets, sidewalks, bike, and bus lanes. A few key areas, like 17th St from Park to Piney Branch, the intersection of Oak and Ogden, and the intersection of Monroe and Brown need attention from DDOT. If reelected, I will continue to advocate for these much-needed improvements and push our agencies to ensure that community voices are heard in planning for safety streets and sidewalks.
column51: The most important thing an ANC commissioner can do is get out and listen to community members. In my first year, I developed a robust listserv to reach residents across my single member district. I send monthly emails and annually, hand-deliver a letter to every single resident. I’ve had countless coffees and long walks with neighbors to talk through challenges they were facing and ensure their voices are heard. To make decisions, I talk through different concepts with neighbors and get a sense of what’s important to them. No issue is easy and residents will always have different perspectives. However, I’m committed to being accessible and responsive to everyone who has an idea to share.
column52: In my first ANC term, I learned so much about this role and continue to learn new things every single day. I’ve built relationships with my fellow commissioners, community-based organizations, local businesses, and District agencies. These relationships help me to get things done for my single member district. In my first term, I passed resolutions to support improving public trash pickup, increasing traffic and sidewalk safety, and reforming vacant and blighted property laws. As a commission, we started the first committees in ANC1D, a Committee of the Whole to improve admin processes and a Public Safety Committee, in response to rising crime District-wide. I think I am the best person to serve in this role because I have a proven track record of working tirelessly to get things done for this community.

column8: I support increasing density throughout ANC 1E. 1E is centered on Georgia Avenue, which has two of the highest ridership Metrobus routes. Most of 1E is within a 5 minute walk of Georgia Avenue and within a 10 minute walk of a green line Metro station. As such, Georgia Avenue (and nearby streets) can support higher population density. Earlier this year, I advocated for upzoning southern portions of Georgia Avenue up to MU-10 in a map amendment case brought forward by Howard University (ZC 24-01). If re-elected, I will advocate for more of Georgia Avenue to be upzoned to MU-10 and to allow for at least 6-plexes to be built by right in surrounding primarily residential neighborhoods.     DC continues to be an exciting place to live, and more and more people will want to live here. If we do not increase density throughout the District, housing prices will continue to accelerate, and DC will be unaffordable to even more people.    Relatedly, I will push back against any further attempts to designate large areas of our neighborhoods as historic districts, and I will push to limit the scope of HPRB. While this might seem separate, historic preservation can have the effect of preventing future density increases. It is clear that historic preservation has been used in some cases by bad faith actors throughout DC to prevent change in their neighborhood (the Chevy Chase historically preserved parking lot is one such example). As I testified at an oversight hearing on February 29th, 2024, the Council should clarify that height and mass are exclusively under the purview of the Office of Zoning. Additionally, the Council should investigate clarifications for the criteria for historic designation 10 DCMR 201.1 because these criteria are currently overly broad.

column49: In my 2022 questionnaire, I shared that my most frequent trip was to take my cat Boomer to the vet once every other month. I promised that I would find alternative means to get our cat to the vet, and we ended up getting an e-bike to take our cat the 4 miles to Friendship Animal Hospital. The trip is only a few minutes longer and is much more enjoyable for me and our cat!     At this point, I rarely use single-occupancy vehicles and the trips taken do not have any particular pattern. Even when traveling for work, I prioritize public transportation and walking as much as possible.     There are a few things that I have done and promise to continue to do:  1) Advocate to friends and neighbors to consider non-driving modes when possible.   2) Advocate for more bike lanes and bus lanes throughout the District.  3) Continue to be an example of how to get around the District with minimal single-occupancy vehicle trips.
column50: An unfortunately common pattern is the lack of timely response from District agencies. Whether that be getting the Department of Buildings to respond to illegal construction, having DDOT (Department of Transportation) address unworkable pickup/drop off zones for buses for schools, or even getting the Board of Nursing to issue nursing licenses in a timely manner, it is clear that many District agencies are failing to meet the basic needs of our residents. There even continue to be issues with our 911 call center, making it more difficult for police to respond to crime. Roughly half of the time I spend as an ANC is badgering on requests to agencies. It should not take me bumping an email thread 8 times over the course of 3 months to get a response from an agency.  I've had to think outside of the box to help constituents. As an example, I submitted a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request to DDOT, which got them to fix that broken pickup/drop off zone. I have also submitted dozens of FOIAs to ORM and related agencies to understand why "How Am I Driving" requests were not being responded to. I then used that information to advocate at Council oversight hearings to get ORM and agencies to take concerns of residents seriously. I will continue to use all tools that are available to advocate for my constituents. Lastly, I recognize that I alone cannot fix these issues. In my personal capacity, I will continue to advocate for candidates up and down the ballot in DC that recognize the importance of having functional District agencies and are willing to advocate for improvements.
column51: Residents who show up to ANC meetings are not a representative sample of the opinions of a neighborhood or DC as a whole. I've had so many conversations with neighbors that wish they could be involved, but they have children or other commitments that make it difficult to attend ANC meetings. I will always listen to the feedback of meeting attendees and constituents, but that does not mean that I will always vote the way a majority of meeting attendees ask me to.    As a current elected, I do my best to communicate with my constituents through e-newsletters, door knocking, dropping off physical newsletters once every couple of months so they can understand where I stand and provide feedback.     My goal is to stick to campaign promises when voting on matters before the ANC. For example, I am passionate about increasing street safety and reducing deaths from reckless drivers. Anyone voting for me can expect that I will vote in favor of resolutions that will improve street safety.
column52: A couple moved into the neighborhood at the beginning of my first term. I had some leftover scones from a community cleanup, so I walked over to offer some and welcome them. They told me about how they were excited to be in the neighborhood with a nice park and were expecting a child. I ended up introducing them to another neighbor with young kids. Fast forward to me collecting signatures last month, and they wanted to share that they appreciated that intro because that other family are some of their best friends now.    I am proud of the work I have done to get District government to be more responsive to the concerns of constituents. I respond quickly to requests for help and set expectations as to what I can and cannot do. But I am most proud of the work I've done to help build a community in the neighborhood. It isn't perfect, and it isn't solely the result of my work, but it is something that I think is important. I'll never be able to put in 311 requests for every single issue in my immediate neighborhood. It takes many neighbors wanting to help and wanting to improve the area for us to have a big impact. And I'm glad that I can do my little part to help make that happen as a commissioner.

column8: ANC 6/8F (Navy Yard) is already quite a dense neighborhood, which makes it extremely walkable and - in my opinion - a great place to live. I think that the current zoning plan for the area has done a relatively good job encouraging density, generally via high-rise condo/apartment buildings in the western half and townhouses in the eastern half. We do have some surface parking lots where additional housing is on long-term plans, but it hasn’t been built yet due to market conditions (not due to zoning). Having said all that, there is at least one location in my ANC that could support additional density, and we’ve already taken action on it as an ANC. We received a proposal for a residential apartment tower for a small plot of land just south of the USDOT building which would be entirely affordable housing. Before we received the plans for this tower, I probably wouldn’t have given that plot of land - currently just a small grassy area - a second thought. We as an ANC have been supportive of the efforts to properly rezone this parcel to allow the tower to be built. This experience will make me think more creatively in the future about additional efforts to increase density throughout Navy Yard.

I can make a point to take Metro up to Silver Spring instead - at least once the Red Line shutdown ends on August 31!
column50: When I filled out this questionnaire for my first race two years ago, I highlighted crime as the biggest issue in Navy Yard. While crime rates are still too high, I am encouraged by our recent year-over-year statistics and it feels like we’re finally improving from the bad days immediately post-pandemic. This year I’m going to call attention to our persistent problems with the lack of pick-up/drop-off (PUDO) zones in Navy Yard. We have many, many high-rise condo/apartment buildings in Navy Yard, and all of those residents are receiving packages from various delivery services (at an increased rate since the pandemic), not to mention all the various businesses and office buildings that also receive deliveries. There are not nearly enough PUDO zones throughout Navy Yard, which causes delivery trucks of all sizes to park in travel lanes, on top of flex-posts that (insufficiently) protect our bike lanes, and to generally wind up causing unsafe conditions for pedestrians. While this may sound like a nuisance issue, I have seen particularly egregious cases of trucks parking in travel lanes and unloading directly into crosswalks - all it will take is one frustrated driver swerving around one of these trucks and a pedestrian is going to get injured or worse.  As indicated in question #19 (by listing this option highest), I would certainly support an ANC resolution calling for the removal of residential parking spaces throughout Navy Yard to enable short-term parking for delivery drivers. I also will use the influence that we have as an ANC during building design reviews to make sure that new developments are adequately planning for PUDO - we as an ANC recently had a lengthy back-and-forth with the developers of the (aforementioned) planned residential tower south of the USDOT building, and we were able to get them to completely rotate the configuration of the lobby entry because we felt that their original PUDO plan was unrealistic. Finally, I would ensure that community agreements with our local businesses contain language indicating that those businesses will not knowingly receive deliveries from trucks which are parked illegally.
column51: I do not believe that the goal of upholding the District of Columbia’s interests is necessarily in conflict with the goal of upholding the interests of my ANC, and I believe that I will (and do) act to make decisions that are beneficial to both. Every neighborhood is different, but using Navy Yard as an example, we are not only a community of 10,000 residents but we’re also a “destination neighborhood” that is frequently visited by other residents of DC, as well as Maryland and Virginia. I would like to see fewer parking spaces on our roads, both because I believe we need to make room for additional PUDO zones, but also because I’d like to see fewer trips made into Navy Yard by car. Encouraging those from Maryland and Virginia to come to Nats games via Metro - instead of via car - is an example of something that benefits both DC as a whole and Navy Yard as a neighborhood.
column52: I’ve heard from many current and former ANC Commissioners that you really start to “hit your stride” during your second term. I’m currently running for my second term, and I can certainly see why this would be the case. There’s definitely a learning curve to this role in figuring out how to navigate all the processes, learning about the DC government agencies that are most relevant to the day-to-day concerns that you hear from residents, and trying to determine how best to be effective given a particular problem. Not to mention that ANC 6/8F was a completely new ANC post-2022-redistricting, so my first year was quite wrapped up in a lot of mundane bureaucratic issues (particularly because I’m the Secretary) such as establishing a bank account, finding office space and a place to hold public meetings, and acquiring the equipment necessary to host virtual meetings. Since all that has been completed - and since I’ve learned my way around various agencies - I feel like I’ve been more effective in this role during the past six months than during my entire first year. I’d love to have the opportunity to continue serving my community at this pace for another two years going forward!

column49: The only trip I take almost exclusively by car is when I get my haircut. It’s a 10-minute drive with nearby free parking. Otherwise, it would be a 15-minute CaBi ride (if I can find a CaBi bike first of all), and then a 10-minute walk because there is no nearby CaBi station. The convenience factor favors driving 99/100 times (especially in the rainy season). I can easily change this trip-mode if I wanted to. I could take a non CaBi bike or even a scooter for this trip.
column50: We're lucky to have a stadium in our neighborhood that’s easily accessible by foot, bike, Metro, or car (including rideshares). However, the increase in car traffic during game or event days brings negative side effects to the surrounding community, including safety concerns, illegal parking, temporary double parking, hazardous rideshare pick-up and drop-off zones, and overall traffic congestion. Given the frequency of games and events at the stadium, this is an ongoing issue, not just a one-time occurrence. There is opportunity to address some of these issues.  We must work more closely with DDOT, DFHV, and other relevant agencies to enhance pedestrian safety by implementing one-way streets, increasing traffic enforcement (such as ticketing, booting, and towing), and expanding alternative mode-share options to ease the strain on neighborhood streets. It's crucial to encourage more visitors to use car-free transportation for accessing games and events. This can be accomplished by investing in alternative transportation options, enforcing traffic regulations, and, most importantly, effectively promoting and communicating these options to the public.
column51: I believe in making decisions based on transparency and accessibility. Agenda and meeting notes should be easily accessible to all residents so that everyone is informed about ongoing and upcoming issues and can provide feedback. Recognizing that some people cannot attend the monthly meetings, I encourage residents to share their concerns via email or during informal office hours. I will use community feedback, along with available data, to guide my decisions.
column52: A Commissioner serves as an advocate for their constituents. With a solid background in urban and transportation planning, I have extensive experience in community outreach and public engagement across various projects. My familiarity with navigating local government bureaucracies equips me with the expertise needed to effectively represent the community. I am deeply passionate about enhancing our urban environment and making sure it is livable for everyone. I am committed to understanding the concerns of my constituents and working diligently with local government agencies and private entities to address them.

column50: I think the biggest issue in my neighborhood is the excess of space devoted to car traffic on Connecticut Ave, which results in huge traffic jams during rush hour, and makes the neighborhood more dangerous to cyclists and pedestrians. I am in favor of reducing our dependency on cars in ANC 3C.  Work with the city, including the city council and DDOT, to find ways of improving road safety for all residents. This could include encouraging alternate forms of transportation by improving bus service and revisiting the issue of protected bike lanes on Connecticut, as well as more small-scale fixes like daylighting intersections and lengthening crosswalk times.
column51: While I would ultimately be responsible for making the decisions I think are best for the neighborhood and the District, I would love to spend as much time as possible hearing from members of the community, especially before    making a major decision. I think it's important to stay connected with people, so I would try and keep up with residents, tenants' associations, and the business community to try and identify blind spots, or factors that I may not have considered.
column52: I love Cleveland Park. I think we have a great community here, and it's been exciting seeing the improvements in the neighborhood over the last couple years - the construction of the pedestrian promenade on Connecticut and the push to remove the restaurant cap come to mind. I think, though, that there's still room for improvement. We have great opportunities here to build more housing, reduce our dependence on cars, and bring more business to the neighborhood. I think I'm the right person to serve as ANC commissioner because if elected, I will do my best to keep improving our neighborhood and make Cleveland Park an even better place to live.

column50: After speaking with residents in my SMD, I feel that the biggest issue in my neighborhood is waste management and rodent control. On any given day, between 1-3 deceased rats can be found on the sidewalk of the street I live on, which is a health and safety hazard. The rats jump out of trash bins at residents, and have eaten holes in car wires, outdoor furniture and grills. In some cases, rats have begun to make homes in residents’ apartments. The District’s Department of Health currently works to fix this issue by deploying rat poison for two weeks at a time, but this is a short-term mitigation of the issue, rather than a long-term solution. My position is that the District and its agencies need to take action to provide additional waste management options for residents that allow for more efficient food scrap disposal. By eliminating food waste and scraps from household trash bins, we can likely cut down on the food supply of the rodents causing issues on our streets.  Work with my fellow commissioners to request that the Department of Public Works expand the pilot compost program to accommodate a greater number of residents and lead an effort to notify residents in my neighborhood of DPW’s new food waste drop-off program and the opportunity to provide feedback on the program.
column51: I will work to make myself as available as possible to the residents I represent by attending public events (e.g. Farmer’s Market every Saturday) in my ANC and SMD and tabling or making an effort to introduce myself and speak to residents who may also be in attendance. By making myself visible on a weekly basis and being present in public areas where residents of my neighborhood already are, I hope to give those I represent a convenient, low-barrier way to talk about what they need to live comfortably in the District. I hope to use the information I get from residents in this way to make decisions that make the District the best it can be. In addition to working to make myself available to residents for in-person discussion, I will use information shared by residents via social media, email, phone call, and text message as resources for informed decision-making. I will also research what other cities like ours may be doing to combat similar issues we may face as a way to ensure I’m making educated decisions that are in the District’s and my neighborhood’s best interests.
column52: I love the District and my neighborhood, and I want to take an active role in making them places where every person who wants to live here can do so and thrive. I’m an excellent communicator with strong organizational skills, and I’ll work diligently to ensure residents in my neighborhood have what they need to live happily and comfortably.

column50: Engaging with NPS on public safety issues; Getting a consistent answer / rulings from the Old Georgetown Board and giving away publicly owned space to select few building owners for streateries that clog traffic, force bikers and scooters to compete with cars for space, and increase greenhouse gas generation since cars can't maintain close to 20 mph due to bumper to bumper traffic  I communicate frequently with NPS representatives to coordinate public safety efforts.  I already communicate with OGB in a way to try to get a better sense of transparency and as well ask for justifications and commitments from the local BID for streateries use that benefits our community at large.  On the latter issue, I wished the city's Public Space Committee would have been more transparent with their rulings and not give away valuable public space for a period of years so easily.  That space could have been used for dedicated bus lanes, bike lanes, revenue generating street parking, etc.  But it goes to a few building owners to benefit restaurants who are no longer struggling post covid--they're just getting more tables in spring/fall and don't keep up with using them during winter and very hot summer days.
column51: I strive for consistency in all my engagement and decision making with residents and the District.  When I make decisions to conduct outreach or to vote on an issue, I always think how that one decision is consistent with how I made decisions for other cases and as well, whether it is consistent with what I tell my neighbors about how I approach problem solving similar issues.
column52: I listen to my residents to understand their needs while also have a strong grasp of the District government, its policymakers and influencers, and therefore its ability to perform certain functions and not others (e.g., federal government control or political logjam/complexities).  This allows me to  be effective in managing expectations while pushing for change as much as I can, whether it is in small increments or on a larger scale.  I think a common mistake elected officials make is overpromising due to a lack of understanding of what they can do and where they can push to make a difference.  When I push, I don't give up on my efforts regardless of how long it may take for me to get result on behalf of my neighbors.  Commissioners are most  influential and impactful when they have neighbors who are willing to be cc'd on emails and correspondence to show that there's a groundswell of support.  We act as their voice at the hyper level of governance and can't forget that when we perform our functions daily.

column50: the negative externalities of living near a major nightlife area and I believe the police and the city need to do more to deter crime, reduce noise pollution, and clean up trash while ensuring our area continues to be a popular and welcoming destination for visitors.  work with the police and advocate for more patrols in underserved areas of my neighborhood where car break-ins are common. I would also like to talk with my fellow commissioners and hold meetings to help create a business improvement district in my area. A BID could significantly improve the relationship between businesses and the neighborhood. Additionally, I would like to work with city officials to increase trash pick up and cleaning of my neighborhood. For example, I would advocate for public safety ambassadors to assist in clean-up efforts and  talk with party-goers to discourage littering and reduce amplified sound that violates noise ordinances. Lastly, I would also put my support behind a proposal that would install cameras to fine cars for being too loud.
column51: As commissioner, I will strive to gather information from a diversity of sources (constituents inside and outside of my district, academic literature, stakeholders, other commissioners) and then make my own decision about what I think will be best for both the District of Columbia and for my ANC. I have a unique responsibility to give a voice to the people of ANC 1B02. I also recognize that localities can create roadblocks to needed investments, and it is important to recognize the benefits to not just others, but also our own neighborhood by allowing these citywide investments to go through. If each neighborhood in DC looks beyond its local concerns and does its part to increase the housing stock, reduce carbon emissions, and increase opportunity and affordability, then DC, the neighborhood, and its residents will be much better off. In this spirit, as ANC I will make decisions by strongly weighing the interests of my neighbors who I directly represent and by also considering the interests of the city as whole that will be impacted by my decisions. I hope to find solutions that are win-win as much as possible.
column52: I believe I am the right person to serve as ANC commissioner because I will bring down the temperature at meetings and collaborate with residents, businesses, developers, and city officials to make my neighborhood more accessible and welcoming to people of all income levels and backgrounds. As a young person who has lived in large cities my whole life, I am excited to be a part of the generational progress happening on land use, transportation, housing, and racial justice. Commissioners have limited power, but they play a critical role in connecting people to their government and helping shape the future of their neighborhood. I think that as someone new, open-minded, and willing to compromise, I can bridge divides in my SMD and effectively advocate for my neighbors and for the progress that must be made at the local level to create a sustainable future.

column8: ANC 5B is uniquely positioned for increased density, thanks to its strategic location bookended by two Metro stations: Brookland and Rhode Island Avenue-Brentwood. These Metro stops, particularly on the Red Line, offer some of the most connected transit options in the city, making them ideal hubs for new housing and retail developments.    The industrial areas bordering the railroad tracks present prime opportunities for thoughtful redevelopment that can introduce more housing while maintaining, and even enhancing, the neighborhood’s character. Key corridors like Rhode Island Avenue, 12th Street, and Monroe Street are ripe for higher density projects. By focusing on areas directly adjacent to the Metro stations, we can encourage development that supports sustainable urban living, reduces reliance on cars, and fosters vibrant, walkable communities.    Increasing density in these areas not only addresses the growing demand for housing but also strengthens the local economy by attracting new retail and services, enhancing the overall quality of life in ANC 5B.

column49: Reflecting on my previous survey responses, I committed to reducing my car usage, particularly for short trips like taking my daughter to and from daycare, which is less than a mile away. I had initially wanted to bike her there but was apprehensive due to dangerous drivers. Fast forward to today, and I’m proud to share that I’ve replaced nearly all of our ANC 5B trips with bike rides. Although I still remain cautious around drivers, I meticulously plan our routes (now to school rather than daycare) to minimize car interactions, opting for protected bike lanes even if it means taking the long way. My daughter loves it so much that she protests whenever we have to use the car, even for out-of-state trips!    A huge shout-out to ANC 5C07 Commissioner Kapur for his invaluable guidance on all things e-biking with a child, and to the wonderful ANC 5B family biking community for their support. It’s a fantastic community to be part of.    Looking ahead, I want to push myself further in two specific ways. First, I’d like to get comfortable biking beyond our immediate neighborhood. Currently, I stick to the MBT or the safe(ish) streets of Brookland. However, I’m hesitant to tackle areas like the intersection near Union Market, where the MBT ends and the chaotic Florida Ave/New York Ave area begins. My daughter’s swim class is just 1.4 miles away, and I commit to trying that route—likely by myself first—so that we can replace our weekly car trip to swim class with the bike.    Secondly, my husband, who has historically been the Capital Bikeshare enthusiast in our family, doesn’t yet have a personal bike. This means that when he joins us, we often end up driving, much to the chagrin of our 4-year-old. I’m committed to convincing him to get his own bike this year so that he can replace the majority of his short trips around the District with bike rides. As for my other trips, such as commuting to work, I generally use the Metro or bus when there are not significant delays (though there seems to be more and more of those lately). The only exception is late-night travel, where I opt for Ubers for safety reasons.
column50: The biggest issue in our neighborhood right now is traffic safety. Because of our location, right next to Maryland and surrounded by major roads like South Dakota Avenue and Rhode Island Avenue, our streets have become a popular cut-through for drivers looking to avoid traffic jams. Unfortunately, a lot of these drivers don’t just cut through—they speed through, blowing past stop signs and traffic lights without a second thought. This is especially worrying in a community like ours, where we have so many young kids and seniors.  To make matters worse, there’s a lack of coordination between DC, Maryland, and Virginia when it comes to holding unsafe drivers accountable. So, even though we have things like stop signs and speed cameras, they don’t always do much to deter the worst offenders—people who just don’t seem to care about anyone’s safety but their own.  I share the frustration of many neighbors who see cars speeding through our streets with little regard for safety. That’s why I’m committed to working alongside [a majority of] my fellow ANC 5B commissioners and Councilmember Zachary Parker to keep this issue front and center with DDOT and the Mayor’s Office. And to remind the Mayor that decisions like her choice not to pursue congestion pricing directly impact communities like ours, where cars not only speed through our neighborhoods but also block our streets by using them as free Metro parking. We need real action to address the traffic problems that are putting our community at risk.  I’m also focused on practical solutions that make an immediate difference. For example, while we push for better enforcement and accountability, I’m a firm believer in physical interventions like bumpouts and speed bumps. These measures force drivers to slow down, not because they suddenly care about safety, but because they don’t want to damage their cars. It’s a simple but effective way to protect our streets and make them safer for everyone, especially our kids and seniors.
column51: As an ANC commissioner, I recognize that Washington, DC is a uniquely transient city. People move in and out frequently, and while I won’t be able to hear from every resident during my term, it’s crucial to keep a finger on the pulse of what’s happening—not just in our community, but also across the District and in other cities facing similar challenges.    Future-proofing is at the heart of how I approach decision-making. It’s about balancing the needs and comforts of our neighbors today with the safety, well-being, and sustainability of our community tomorrow. This requires a broad perspective, drawing on a cross-section of information—from listening to my neighbors and following Council meetings to staying current on District news and studying trends and best practices from other cities.    DC has the unique advantage of learning from the successes and challenges of other cities that have implemented similar projects or policies. For example, if we want to understand the long-term impact of a new infrastructure project, we can look to a city like Chicago, which might have implemented a similar initiative 15 years ago. By analyzing what worked and what didn’t, we can adapt those insights to better serve our own community’s needs.    My goal is to make informed decisions that aren’t just reactive to today’s issues but are also forward-thinking, ensuring that the investments we make now—whether in buildings, infrastructure, or community planning—will stand the test of time. We don’t want to spend millions on a project that becomes obsolete in a decade. Instead, we should strive for solutions that will serve both our current residents and those who will call DC home in the future.    In this way, while I may not hear from every single constituent, I can still represent the best interests of our community and the District as a whole by making decisions that are thoughtful, informed, and future-oriented.
column52: I believe I’m the right person to serve as an ANC commissioner because I genuinely care about my neighbors and our community. But let me be clear—there’s nothing particularly special about me that makes me more qualified than anyone else. In fact, I’ve had to Google more things than I can count and ask plenty of “dumb” questions along the way. The truth is, most of what I do as an ANC is common sense, and it’s the kind of thing that I believe many of my neighbors would do if they were in my position.    One of my goals is to share what I’ve learned and, hopefully, build a pipeline of future ANC commissioners from our community. I’m just keeping the seat warm for now, and I plan on passing the torch to one of the many fantastic neighbors we have in ANC 5B who also love and care about this amazing community.    This role isn’t about power—it’s about service. Anyone who thinks otherwise doesn’t really understand what being an ANC is all about. One of the best parts of this job is that I get to show my daughter what it means to be involved in the community and to do for others. Plus, I’ve met so many wonderful people through my work as an ANC - it's incredible. The friendships I’ve made and the connections I’ve built are truly priceless.    So yes, I’m here to serve, but I’m also here to encourage others to step up and get involved. Our community is full of amazing people, and I’m excited to see who will take on this role next.

column8: While Columbia Heights is already one of the densest neighborhoods in DC, I believe there are still significant opportunities to increase density and accommodate more housing across ANC 1A, particularly along 14th Street. This would require some redevelopment of existing and older low-density properties.    In my SMD, I see potential for increased density directly on 14th Street, especially between Monroe and Spring, where several properties could be replaced with high-density, mixed-income housing. That said, I believe that a mix of high-density and single family homes is what makes Columbia Heights so great. As we move from 14th Street towards areas like Holmead Place and 13th Street, I can envision more duplexes and triplexes and still preserve many existing single-family homes, creating a balanced mix of housing types.    Looking at the broader ANC area, there are even more opportunities:    1) Columbia Heights Village near 14th and Columbia Road: This important and historic affordable housing property occupies a large piece of land but is currently low-density. Redeveloping this area could significantly increase the number of housing units, particularly for lower-income individuals and families, while also creating new retail opportunities along 14th Street.    2) The 2800 block of 14th Street between Girard and Harvard: Similar to Columbia Heights Village, this area represents another opportunity to transform a large, low-density property into a vibrant, mixed-use development that could house many more residents.    3) This might be a bit controversial, but I think the Giant grocery store on Park Road is a prime location in the heart of Columbia Heights and is underutilized, with just a parking garage above the store. This would be a big undertaking, but redeveloping this site to include apartments above the grocery store could be transformative for the neighborhood, adding significant housing in an area already surrounded by high-density buildings.     4) There is also a vacant lot at 11th Street and Lamont Street that needs to be developed into a mixed-use property with numerous housing units and retail spaces.    These redevelopment projects represent major opportunities to increase housing density in Columbia Heights while maintaining the unique character of the neighborhood.

column49: I already use public transit (Metro and bus routes 52, 54, and 59) or a bike/scooter for most trips I take in DC. I have a car, but only use it to take my dog to the vet or run errand that are far away. However, sometimes I’ll use Uber or Lyft after a night out when I just want to get home faster. I have already started changing this and commit to using transit, walking, or biking after a night out. The extra few minutes it might take are well worth the emissions saved (and the money saved)!
column50: One of the biggest issues in our neighborhood is the deterioration of public and community gathering spaces across Columbia Heights. We are in need of investment to improve and refurbish public spaces to meet the needs of neighbors and businesses alike. Specifically, the Civic Plaza, sidewalks in the core part of 14th Street, streets, and bike lanes need attention. It has been over 20 years since Columbia Heights has received significant investment to improve these public spaces, and it is time for renewal.  If re-elected, I would continue efforts I have already started working with neighborhood organizations and property owners to put in place new mechanisms to regularly invest in, maintain, and do programming in public spaces. Realistically, as an ANC commissioner, I would: 1) Advocate for increased funding from the city for public space improvements in our area; 2) Collaborate with local businesses and community organizations to develop a plan for regular maintenance and programming of public spaces; 3) Work with DDOT and other relevant agencies to prioritize improvements to sidewalks, streets, and bike lanes in our neighborhood; 4) Keep organizing community clean-up events and encourage local engagement in maintaining our public spaces; 5) Continue to push for creative solutions to address vacant properties, which often contribute to the deterioration of public spaces.
column51: As an ANC commissioner, I strive to make decisions that balance the needs of my immediate constituents with the broader interests of the District. One of the things I have tried very hard to do during my first term as ANC is to remind myself of the voices not in the room at an ANC meeting or the emails I'm not receiving because of neighbors in Columbia Heights that may not know about the ANC or understand how to engage. They are still our constituents and neighbors. Here are some concrete things I will do to make informed decisions:    1) Actively seek out diverse perspectives by engaging with various community groups and attending local events.    2) Maintain open lines of communication through regular newsletters, social media updates, and community meetings.    3) Consider the potential impact of decisions on my SMD, the ANC, and the wider District.    4) Stay informed about city-wide issues and initiatives to ensure my decisions align with broader District goals.    5) Collaborate with other ANC commissioners and city officials to gain a more comprehensive understanding of issues.    6) Work to bring neighbors along more as decisions are made both at the ANC and City-wide level, communicating more proactively and clearly, even if I know they might disagree with me or I might disagree with them.
column52: I believe I am the right person to continue serving as an ANC commissioner for my SMD for several reasons:    1) Experience: I have already served one term and have developed a good understanding of the role and its challenges. I'm eager to continue the work I've started and build on the relationships I've established.    2) Business Focus: With my background in business and as a small business owner, I bring a unique perspective to the ANC. I've formed the ANC 1A Small Business & Economic Development committee and rebuilt relationships with business owners, advocating for economic development.    3) Proactive Approach: I'm a doer who takes action to see things move forward. I’m not someone who just likes to talk and plan, I like to take action and see things move forward. I know things often happen slowly at the ANC level, but I’m a big believer that “the squeaky wheel gets the grease” and I plan to push as hard as I can to make 1A better.     4) Advocacy: I've been focused on addressing vacant properties in my area and across the city, passing a resolution calling on the Council to reform how vacant properties are proactively addressed in DC. I also started a city-wide ANC Vacant Property Caucus to bring ANCs together on this topic and advocate collectively.    5) Communication: I believe in keeping constituents informed and engaged. My monthly newsletters and efforts to increase community involvement demonstrate my commitment to transparent and inclusive representation.    6) Vision for Improvement: I have a clear vision for improving our neighborhood and I am eager to share that vision with neighbors, continue building upon it and shaping it with their input, and being vocal to make it a reality one step at a time.

column8: My single member district of 7D05 encompasses the 174 acre RFK Campus which represents a once in a generation opportunity to build much needed housing to ease our city's housing shortage. Benning Road also has vacant lots that can be utilized. I also believe that converting vacant or under-utilized office buildings downtown into housing would also be ideal.

column8: We have several areas in Ward 8 with a lot of underutilized land and vacant properties, particularly along Martin Luther King Jr. Avenue SE, Alabama Avenue SE, Mississippi Avenue SE and South Capitol Street SE. These spots are ideal for increasing density because they’re well-connected with infrastructure and public transportation. By focusing development here, we can create mixed-use spaces that include low income NOT affordable housing, retail, and community areas. This will help revitalize our local economy while ensuring that our long-time residents can stay in their homes. Currently, with the rise of rental costs most families can no longer afford to stay in their homes for 10 years at a time. Under the current condition they will be moving every five years. But as we pursue this growth, it's imperative that we hold developers accountable. We need strong commitments to low income housing and community benefits agreements so that new developments truly serve all of our residents, especially those who are most vulnerable to being displaced. Our goal should be to build a more inclusive and equitable Ward 8, where everyone can thrive.

column51: My approach to decision-making is rooted in the principles of equity, justice, and transparency. While I am deeply committed to the well-being of my single-member district, I recognize that my role is to serve the broader interests of the entire District of Columbia.    Given that it’s impossible to hear from every single constituent, I would prioritize active and consistent engagement with the community, ensuring that I am accessible and open to diverse perspectives. This includes hosting regular community meetings, both in-person and virtually, to create multiple channels for residents to share their concerns and ideas.    When making decisions, I would carefully weigh the input from my constituents alongside the broader needs and values of the District. My background as an activist and lawmaker, combined with my experience in successfully lobbying Congress, has taught me the importance of advocating for policies that uplift the most vulnerable while ensuring fairness and justice for all.    I would approach each decision with a focus on long-term impact, always asking whether the outcome will advance the well-being of all residents, particularly those who have been historically marginalized. By keeping an open mind, listening to a wide range of voices, and staying true to the principles that guide me, I will make decisions that reflect not just the interests of my SMD, but the collective good of the entire District.
column52: I am confident that I am the right person to serve as an ANC Commissioner for my Single Member District because I bring a bold and unwavering commitment to breaking the monotony that currently exists and prevents the fair treatment of all people in Ward 8. With three terms of experience as a Commissioner, I have consistently taken a stand for justice and equity, challenging the status quo and advocating for meaningful change.    In 2022, I successfully lobbied Congress with my partners, resulting in the dismissal of the revised DC Criminal code—a testament to my ability to effect change at the highest levels. As someone who writes laws for the betterment of all people, I bring a deep understanding of the legislative process and a passion for crafting policies that uplift our community.    I have been an activist long before I even knew what it meant, dedicating my life to working on behalf of the people of Ward 8. My experience, passion, and proven track record of advocacy make me uniquely qualified to continue serving as an ANC Commissioner, where I will keep fighting for the rights and well-being of our residents.

column8: There are plenty of unused or underutilized spaced in my ANC where housing density can be increased. Specifically, at Sun Trust Plaza, we'll be seeing development soon that should bring necessary housing to the community. I think it makes sense to look at the southern and eastern portions of our ANC as well, as between the Columbia Road redevelopment project and the revitalization of Sun Trust Plaza, there will be a great deal of construction around 18th Street and Columbia Road for the next few years, and spreading out some of the development would likely be prudent.    Additionally, a quick note on the "Affordable Housing" section below. I think many of those descriptors CAN be tied to both affordable and market rate housing, but are not necessarily fundamental to either. Ie, subsidized or rent controlled housing can be affordable housing, but affordable housing is not inherently subsidized or rent controlled housing. To be affordable housing, I don't think it need be rent-controlled, but I do think rent-controlled housing is likely to be affordable. To avoid mischaracterizing my thoughts, I'm providing a minimal answer, but I'm happy to follow up with additional details if you can offer some clarity on how this will be interpreted!

column8: In Ward 7, many of our major corridors are underdeveloped. In ANC 7B, these corridors include places like Pennsylvania Avenue, S.E., and Minnesota Avenue, S.E. These corridors are prime spaces for mixed-use development that can bring both increased housing density to ANC 7B and expanded the availability of commercial space, providing much-needed amenities such as grocery stores, restaurants, and other entertainment options to ANC 7B and Ward 7 at large. We have already seen some housing density increases along these corridors, however, we have not yet seen much mixed-use development along these corridors.    In 2020, prior to my tenure as a commissioner, I became a founding member of the Pennsylvania Avenue East Community Coalition (PAECC). This organization was formed by community members representing neighborhoods along the Pennsylvania Avenue corridor in ANC 7B and successfully advocated for the creation of a Main Street program along the corridor. That same year, the council funded our request, and the following year the Office of Planning launched a small area plan covering the Pennsylvania Avenue corridor in ANC 7B. Since then, the plan's implementation has begun. We have seen some new businesses move in, and developers start to purchase commercial properties for redevelopment. I support mixed-use development and increased density in these spaces and will continue to work with developers to advocate for utilizing properties on these corridors to increase density and provide retail space for future community amenities.

column49: When filling out this questionnaire in 2022, I committed to defaulting to public transit to get to the airport when it is operating and only using taxis when traveling to or from the airport outside of public transit hours. I have lived up to this commitment and have been using MetroRail when going to DCA or IAD, and the MARC commuter rail when going to BWI. This choice has not only been great for the environment, but it also costs me less money and means I won’t get stuck in traffic in a taxi or rideshare on my way to the airport.    Additionally, despite there not being a convenient transit path from my home, I have been defaulting to using MetroBus to Circulator or MetroBus to DC Streetcar to get to Union Station. Earlier this year, I authored a resolution (7B-24-03), which unanimously passed ANC 7B and provided WMATA with comprehensive feedback and recommendations on the proposed Better Bus changes as they pertain to ANC 7B. Among those recommendations, we asked for a bus route that would provide several ANC 7B communities a one-seat bus ride to Union Station, the largest transit hub in the District, and for the restoration of our bus connection to the DC Streetcar, which a bus route change would eliminate for 7B residents.    Earlier this year, DDOT installed a new Capital Bikeshare station on the border of 7B03, which I have been utilizing since it is only a couple of blocks from my home. However, I feel I could be getting more use out of it. With that in mind, this year I am committing to using Bikeshare more often when traveling within ANC 7B for meetings, on-site visits, and for short nearby errands such as returning books to the library.
column50: In 2022, when asked this question, the top issues I named were traffic calming on residential streets, NPS property maintenance, and businesses not properly maintaining their properties. I think we still have a ways to go in terms of traffic calming, but I have successfully fostered a relationship with NPS to receive better service for their properties in 7B03. This year, I would also add building and construction neglect from DC agencies to this list.  There is a property in our community where residents are rightfully scared. A new owner has taken control of the property and has been renovating it in unsafe ways, ignoring stop-work orders from the Department of Buildings. Earlier this year, after months of residents sounding alarm bells, this building experienced a fire. What residents said was going to happen tragically did, and yet since the fire, not much has changed. Stop-work orders continue to be ignored, and residents consistently live in fear that another tragedy is just around the corner.  To help the residents of the building noted above, I have listened to them when they felt no one else was. As an ANC Commissioner, my ability to force change is limited, and the building residents understand this, but that doesn't mean I am without a voice. I feel strongly that the District government is failing to protect residents of this building and our neighborhood at large when stop-work orders are being ignored without penalty. I have and will continue to work to ensure vulnerable residents in 7B03 are not ignored.  I will continue to reach out to the Office of the Attorney General, the Office of Buildings, and Councilmembers who wield influence over these agencies to ensure that not only are these residents' concerns being heard, but that promises are followed through on so they can feel safe inside their own homes. I have helped bring several representatives from these agencies to our community to see firsthand what is happening at this building, and I will continue working with these agencies until the problems are resolved.  Additionally, although the work is not complete, I believe I have helped make strides in addressing residents' complaints about speeding and NPS property maintenance in 7B03. If reelected, I also intend to build on these successes and continue fostering relationships with local and federal agencies so everyone’s voice can be heard and resolutions reached in a timely manner.
column51: When making decisions, it is important to hear from the people most impacted. Although I try to post ANC happenings on my official social media channels, I understand that it only reaches so many people. When an issue arises that directly impacts a particular street or block, I typically canvas the affected area to hear directly from the most impacted residents. Additionally, I always keep channels of communication open, which is why I have chosen to publish my phone number and email address online so residents can always reach me. Furthermore, I host SMD walks several times per year, inviting neighbors to join me as I walk a section of 7B03 to hear directly from them about what could be improved in their immediate area.
column52: During my final year of college, I was elected to the County Board of Supervisors, where I represented the residents of our county's 13th supervisory district. Since 2016, I have served on the board of the Randle Highlands Citizens’ Civic Association, and since 2021, I have represented ANC 7B03, which previously included parts of the Dupont Park, Fairlawn, Randle Highlands, and Twining communities. Last year, the district was significantly redrawn to consolidate Randle Highlands into a single SMD, 7B03.    During my tenure serving 7B03, I believe I have been responsive to the communities that entrusted me to be their voice and have successfully addressed the issues raised during my time as their commissioner. I am ready and well-positioned to continue working for the community I love, helping to improve the lives of people in 7B03, Ward 7, and Washington, D.C. at large.

column49: One trip that I can commit to taking by foot or by bike, instead of driving, is my visit to the grocery store. However, it's important to emphasize that there are currently no grocery stores or restaurants in my neighborhood. This lack of essential services within walking or biking distance forces me to rely on my vehicle. The inequitable development in my area has left it underserved, and without access to these basic amenities, I am unable to make this shift at present. If the District government is truly committed to remedying these injustices, I am hopeful that my neighborhood will see the development of grocery stores and restaurants within a walkable or bikeable distance. This would allow me to reduce my reliance on my vehicle and contribute to the city’s goal of becoming carbon-free by 2050.
column50: The biggest issue in my neighborhood is the lack of development and retail options, coupled with the general neglect of city services such as street cleaning, timely responses to 311 requests, and the persistent problem of illegal dumping. This neglect creates a cycle where the neighborhood becomes less attractive to private developers, who then hesitate to invest in bringing much-needed businesses and services to the area. The excuse often given is that private developers don't see the potential or profitability in our neighborhood, but the reality is that if the city itself doesn’t step up to provide the basic services that every community deserves, it only exacerbates the problem. My position is that this neglect is unacceptable and contributes directly to the inequities we face as a community. While I recognize the limited scope of commissioners’ and commissions’ authority, I would focus on advocating fiercely for more consistent and equitable city services.  If elected, I would also work to foster stronger partnerships between the community, the city, and potential developers. By improving the basic infrastructure and services, we can make our neighborhood more attractive to private investment. This would involve not just advocacy but also collaboration with other commissioners, city officials, and community organizations to ensure that our neighborhood receives the attention and resources it deserves. My goal would be to create a more livable, vibrant community that attracts the kind of development that residents want and need.
column51: As an active member of my community, both living and working in the district, I see the challenges we face as my own. I believe that to make informed and equitable decisions as an ANC commissioner, it's crucial to actively listen and engage with constituents, understanding how the decisions I’m tasked with making will impact the diverse groups within our community. This means that while I may not hear directly from every constituent during my term, I would ensure that my actions reflect a commitment to equity and the overall well-being of the community. Additionally, I would make my decision-making process as transparent as possible, explaining the rationale behind my choices and how they align with the interests of both my SMD and the District. This includes being clear about how I weigh different factors and the potential impacts on various segments of the population.
column52: I believe I am the right person to serve as an ANC commissioner for SMD: 7C06 because Washington, D.C., is not just where I live—it's my home. This neighborhood is where I chose to settle, make an investment, and build my life. I am deeply committed to seeing this community thrive, and I want to share in the fruits of a prosperous and vibrant neighborhood with my fellow residents.

column8: Our Commission is fortunate to be along major transit corridors: the red line between NoMA and Brookland/CUA, North Capitol Street, the Metropolitan Branch Trail, and Rhode Island Avenue NE. Density along these corridors can (and should) be increased. By waiving parking minimums, we can improve the financial feasibility of such projects and incentivize the production of affordable housing units. The land along these corridors is underutilized, with large swaths of asphalt and industrially zoned lots.    Outside those corridors, we don’t see enough multifamily buildings in traditionally single-family zoned areas. 2-4 unit conversions on single-family residential lots should be on the table.

column8: Columbia Heights is already one of the city's densest areas, so any increase in density should be strategic, targeting areas that can best accommodate new housing while supporting community development and preventing displacement. Key locations for this increased density and/or new housing should include underutilized and vacant properties, as well as areas along major transportation corridors like 14th Street and Georgia Avenue. The city should consider leveraging more creative, community-driven options, e.g., land banks. This tool could be used to return vacant and tax-delinquent properties to productive use, transforming them into mixed-use and mixed-income developments that integrate affordable housing, community amenities, and commercial spaces.    Additionally, when considering upzoning or increasing density, it's essential to use a broad perspective that accounts for diverse economic and age-specific needs—prioritizing how we use and access community amenities, public spaces, and resources is crucial. Ensuring that our infrastructure keeps pace with increased density and balancing new developments while preserving the ccommunity's needs is critical. This holistic and long-term approach to neighborhood development can go a long way toward preventing displacement and ensuring that all residents benefit from these changes.

column49: I actively avoid driving whenever possible and prioritize public transportation and walking around the city. However, I recognize the importance of further reducing transportation emissions to help the District achieve its carbon-free goal by 2050. This commitment requires a collective effort to transition from single-occupancy vehicle trips to more sustainable modes of transportation and to design our public spaces to promote walkability.     Our public transportation system needs to expand to accommodate nontraditional schedules and look beyond typical commuter traffic. Many residents, including myself, have varying work hours, destinations that fall outside the typical rush hour times and routes, and responsibilities that require reliable and accessible transit options at all times. Furthermore, improving public transportation safety is crucial. This is true for everyone, but especially for the many young people who travel outside their neighborhoods and wards across the city during regular school hours and for after-school activities. Expanding and improving public transportation services makes it more enticing for everyone to choose sustainable transportation methods, regardless of their schedule or where they need to be at any hour of the day.
column50: The biggest issue in my neighborhood is the lack of holistic and integrated solutions to the complex challenges our community faces. Our lived experiences do not occur in vacuums, so we need to stop approaching solutions as one-offs without considering the bigger picture. Unfortunately, siloing can often lead to temporary fixes rather than sustainable progress, and as leaders, we need to recognize when we get something wrong or fail to truly engage and incorporate the perspectives of those most impacted by policies. Take public safety for our young people as an example. Ensuring their safety requires more than just policing; it involves creating safe environments both in and out of school, providing mental health support, and ensuring safe passage routes. The Safe Passage Program is a great initiative, but it has to be part of a broader strategy that includes community engagement, educational support, and out-of-school time opportunities. Creating this public safety ecosystem means coordinating efforts across different sectors —schools (public charters and traditional), law enforcement, local businesses, community organizations, families, and caregivers. It requires a strong network of support that addresses the root causes of safety concerns and promotes overall well-being in a way that reflects how our young people live and what they need.  By fostering collaboration and engaging coalitions and networks of trusted partners, I actively seek out comprehensive strategies that address the multifaceted nature of our neighborhood's issues. If elected, I will continue to ensure that our neighborhood's solutions are interconnected and that we are constantly looking at the bigger picture, advocating for integrated strategies that address the underlying causes of our challenges and leverage the strengths of our community.
column51: In my previous terms as an ANC commissioner, I have demonstrated a balanced approach to decision-making that considers both the specific needs of my single-member district and the broader interests of the District of Columbia. My experience has taught me the importance of building and maintaining strong relationships with key stakeholders, including residents, community organizations, and government officials. Part of my role is proactively seeking diverse perspectives by meeting people where they are and fostering connections.    In situations with conflicting interests, I have historically employed a solution-oriented approach, striving to find common ground that tries to balance the needs of different groups. I have demonstrated my investment in building relationships across various sectors and have continuously worked to facilitate collaborative efforts aimed at solutions that balance the needs of our residents with thoughtful policies.
column52: My work with youth and other successful initiatives, such as creating an urban communal farm from an abandoned parcel, demonstrate my commitment to innovative and community-driven solutions. A commitment to the greater good and the long-term well-being of our community guides my decisions. By upholding the District of Columbia's interests and remaining open to feedback and collaboration, I have served as a representative who listens, understands, and acts in the best interests of all constituents.

column8: To be more specific than everywhere, density should be increased in neighborhoods in upper NW where retail, school and recreation facilities already exist and would benefit greatly from added density.     The District government should also prioritize density in areas where it doesn’t exist as of now: for example: Georgetown, Foxhall, and Palisades, to name but a few. In addition, the District would also benefit from additional density in neighborhoods such as the 14th St corridor, Ivy City, and on main avenues such as Georgia Avenue NW, RI Avenue NW and NE, and North Capitol St NE and NW. The success of mixed-use neighborhoods with density such as the Union Market District and Navy Yard should encourage the District planners to pursue similar projects.    The District government should also speed up the conversion of vacant office buildings in the downtown area to residential housing, and working to encourage businesses such as grocery stores to open downtown to be able to create mixed-use neighborhoods that already have transit access.

column50: The biggest issue in my neighborhood is the lack of safe street infrastructure. There are streets that are designed as and function as dangerous highways. We have non-ADA accessible sidewalks, dangerous intersections, speeding drivers, lack of infrastructure that would slow drivers down, unmarked crosswalks, the list goes on. And nearly every day, there are near-misses that far too often lead to crashes.  My position: this is unacceptable. Our neighborhood deserves access to safe streets, and everyone – no matter their mode of transportation - should be able to navigate our streets safely. We also need to have more transit options, and encourage DDOT to prioritize projects such as a bus priority corridor on Rhode Island Avenue NE/NW and North Capitol St NE/NW.  While the commissions’ authority is indeed limited, proactive and sustained engagement with DDOT, as well as passing comprehensive resolutions at the ANC level can indeed yield results that would lead to improving the safety on our streets. For example, as a commissioner, I have introduced (and helped pass) multiple resolutions that prioritized specific blocks and intersections for street safety measures. In my individual capacity as a commissioner, I also advocated for - and helped get install! - a new crosswalk where none existed, a speed hump, sidewalk fixes, and getting crosswalks repainted where only a single painted line existed. These measures, while small, have a demonstrable positive impact on our daily lives. If re-elected, I would: Advocate DDOT to be more bold in its thinking to address traffic safety issues, and continue to advocate and push for changes, whether infrastructure, redesign, traffic pattern changes, to make our streets safer for everyone.
column51: What I’ve learned over the past two years in serving as an ANC commissioner is that we have to think bigger and look beyond short-term gains or losses, and think about how it would better serve the District writ large. Decisions made miles away from my SMD will also impact people in my SMD, and so thinking about how would proposals - like safer streets, or better transit routes - impact not just residents in the SMD, but also how it would benefit or harm future residents and visitors. I find it incredibly helpful to talk to fellow commissioners across wards and share and learn from them - from ways to get a car towed to how to better advocate with the Council and the District government. I also try to keep up to date with the latest research – for example, on issues such as safe streets and learn more about what other cities and countries are doing to ensure everyone has the ability to navigate streets safely.
column52: I’m fortunate to live in a great neighborhood and to be able to call it home for more than a decade now. It feels like a place where you can build a community no matter how long you’ve lived here. That said, there are a lot of challenges that we have to tackle head-on, and my goal is to ensure that our neighborhood benefits from the same attention from D.C. agencies that other parts of the city do - whether that’s equitable and improved access to city services, or improved road designs that make our streets calmer and safer. As an ANC commissioner, I would advocate for making our neighborhood better and brighter not just for current residents, but for all District residents and visitors.

column50: The South Dakota Avenue Corridor Safety Project and my position is that I support this project, making our streets safer, and believe the status quo is not acceptable. column51: In my first term, I've tried my best to make decisions in the interests of both my constituents as well as the entire District by weighing factors such as the local direct impact to neighbors and whether the issue is a question of policy or one of implementation.  I am available to my constituents via email, phone, and individual meeting so folks can directly voice concerns at any time.  I also aim to be as transparent as possible through my website, blog, and monthly newsletter so folks are informed and we minimize surprises.  On key issues I've held single-member district meetings and solicited feedback through surveys, to enable me to hear and understand specific concerns folks have so I can work to resolve them.  At the end of the day, I try to stick to what commitments I've made and expect voters to hold me accountable if the decisions I make aren't the right ones.
column52: In my first term, I was able to "nudge" forward a lot of initiatives I committed to work on when I ran for office, especially for safer streets. column50: I believe the most pressing issue in our neighborhood is the need to improve our green and public spaces. My position on this matter is that enhancing these areas is crucial for several reasons.  Firstly, well-maintained parks and green spaces can significantly reduce our community’s carbon footprint. Trees and greenery act as natural air filters and help in managing urban heat islands, which contribute to better overall environmental health.  Secondly, improving these spaces can help in reducing criminal activity. Studies have shown that well-lit and actively used public spaces tend to deter crime. By creating more inviting and safe environments, we can foster a stronger sense of community.  Moreover, these enhancements provide valuable activities and engagement opportunities for our youth.  Advocate for and work with local stakeholders, including community groups, city planners, and environmental organizations. I would focus on identifying key areas in need of improvement, seeking grants and funding opportunities, and organizing community events to bring attention to these issues. By leveraging partnerships and community support, we can make incremental but meaningful progress in enhancing our neighborhood’s green and public spaces.
