column50: Improving our street safety for all modes of transportation--but especially for pedestrians and bicyclists. This was my #1 priority when I got elected, especially because of Grant Circle traffic safety concerns, and for the past year I've been working closely with DDOT and our Petworth community to finalize a plan to bring safety measures to Grant Circle, which after its implementation in spring 2025, should slow traffic and drastically improve pedestrian and bicyclist visibility. There are other problematic intersections in my SMD which need a similar redesign—such as the Kansas/Allison/8th St NW intersection, which includes five entrances/exits on a hill next to a school, with long crosswalks, a bike lane, and severe visibility issues—and have been pushing DDOT to prioritize this area as one of their next projects.  Continue my strong advocacy of traffic calming measures to create safer streets via the Grant Circle project and Kansas/Allison/8th project by both collecting input from neighbors in public forums and working closely with DDOT. In addition to these bigger traffic calming projects, we also have other streets or intersections that have a major need for speed bumps or flashing lights, and I plan to continue advocating for these traffic calming measures. In addition to traffic calming, I also plan to continue advocating for the improvement of Petworth’s recreation needs, namely replacing the Grant Circle Playground and advising the $19 million renovation of Upshur Park. In early 2023, the National Park Service (NPS) demolished the beloved Grant Circle Playground, leaving it empty with no play equipment and also leaving behind large holes from the uninstalled equipment. Recognizing the community need for this play area, especially for local daycares and nannyshares which still continued to use the empty playground, I started a drive for donations of used outdoor play equipment as a temporary measure for kids to play there while we figured out how to replace the permanent structures. Facing the unique challenge of being DC government trying to help improve federal land (owned by NPS), I did everything I could—asking NPS to fund it, asking DCPS to install discarded playground equipment from a nearby school, asking the Department of Parks and Recreation (DPR) to take over the land to install a playground, and even applying for Congressional earmarks from Eleanor Holmes Norton’s office—and seemingly exhausted every possible avenue. However, I have not given up; we have consistently asked DPR (both formally and via ANC 4C resolution) to create a new nearby playground on DC-owned land to replace this one, especially since most of Petworth is in a “playground desert,” and I plan to continue advocating NPS to budget for and fund the replacement of this playground.  I also plan to continue advising and overseeing the $19 million renovation of Upshur Park. Last fall, DPR asked ANC 4C for our input on how to prioritize this renovation, and ANC 4C unanimously passed a resolution letter calling for this renovation to focus on major improvements to the Upshur pool and pool house, the Upshur dog park, and the Upshur Playground. While Upshur Park is just outside of my SMD, I represent Petworth parents and advocate for their recreation needs, and want to continue seeing this project through fruition by advising community input and questions throughout the renovation.
column51: A good example of how I collect community input to inform our decisions is how we handled DDOT’s proposal to redesign Grant Circle for the overall improvement of traffic safety. In summer 2023, I kept pushing DDOT to install traffic calming measures to improve Grant Circle, and in November 2023 DDOT presented a proposal for how to redesign Grant Circle. Only the top portion of Grant Circle includes my SMD’s constituents, but I was inclusive in my approach to gathering input, so that everyone in ANC 4C (and even in nearby 4D) could have their voices heard. First, I organized a series of public forums on various days and times, with some as virtual options, to provide accessibility to different groups of people with different needs (parents, people who work, seniors, and more). I communicated proposed changes and ways to provide input via my monthly newsletter, and also used the Petworth News blog as an outlet to spread the word. We of course provided updates in our monthly ANC meetings, and eventually decided on an ANC resolution in February 2024 which provided overall support for a Grant Circle redesign, but with caveats of issues we wanted DDOT to consider because of what we heard from residents.   After our resolution, we asked DDOT to come up with three options to re-present to the community, so the community wouldn’t react by either saying yes or no to their proposal, but instead would be forced to pick an option for traffic calming (instead of trying to derail progress and shut down the project). DDOT presented their new plan of three options in June 2024, and it was yet again a contentious meeting, so I decided to learn from our mistakes the first time around and go the extra mile with gathering community input: I created a survey asking neighbors to vote on which of the three proposed DDOT plans they support, and heavily advertised the survey and how to provide formal public comments to DDOT via Petworth News, Twitter, my ANC newsletter, the Petworth listservs, Petworth social media groups, one-on-one emails, messages on WhatsApp, and more.   I had a personal opinion about which plan would be best for our community, but was committed to choosing whichever option the majority voted for. I even created hardcopy forms for senior citizens who are not as tech savvy to vote, with info on the options on one side and the vote form on the back, and dropped those off at the the two churches on Grant Circle, and reached out to everyone at the daycare on Grant Circle as well. We received around 300 survey responses and almost 100 email responses, of which 75% indicated preference for the Alternative 1 plan to shut down one block of 5th St south of Grant Circle to motorists, and make the neighboring block of Illinois Ave NW two-way, so this is what we advocated for in our ANC 4C resolution to DDOT.
column52: I truly love serving as the ANC 4C05 commissioner because I enjoy both connecting with residents by helping with their individual needs and also leading larger projects which will help benefit the community for years to come, like the Grant Circle project, Upshur Park renovation, and hopefully replacing our Grant Circle Playground sometime soon. I have greatly improved communication by instituting a monthly newsletter with important hyperlocal information on government projects, meeting minutes, opportunities for residents, and upcoming neighborhood events. I am quick to respond when neighbors reach out asking for help on everything from replacing residential trash cans or how to exterminate rats to dealing with cars on fire in the alley, and I make myself accessible not just via email but also via social media platforms and in-person community forums. I have great time management skills, as displayed in my able to successfully balance my ANC commissioner position with my full-time federal government job, leading the Petworth Peanuts nonprofit as their Vice President, and of course being a mom to two toddler boys. I am able to do all of this because I care about advocating for meaningful change, especially hyperlocal traffic safety improvements, and am dedicated to serving my community and improving the quality of life for all residents in Petworth.

column8: As a general principal, I would support increasing density throughout ANC 2A with few exceptions. Specifically, there may be opportunities to increase density along Pennsylvania Avenue, and in my first term on the Commission, I supported the proposal to demolish the old Salvation Army building at 2626 Penn NW and replace it with 31 new housing units. I think projects of that nature are a good idea and should be pursued in cases where the existing property is either not utilized or significantly underutilized. At the same time, we are already a relatively dense community, so there are two specific opportunities that I think are most worth highlighting. First, I strongly support the conversion of office space into housing, even in mixed-use scenarios. And second, I’m in favor of completely reimagining how space is used in the western part of the neighborhood (including the areas around the Watergate and the waterfront), including the elimination or reconfiguration of the area’s absurdly complicated and inefficient highway system.

column50: The planned opening of the Aston bridge housing facility at 1129 New Hampshire Ave NW.  I am committed to ensuring this important, necessary housing program is successfully and harmoniously integrated into the community. I’ve been an outspoken advocate for the project since it was announced, imploring my colleagues and neighbors to consider what is morally and ethically right (which, in this case, is prioritizing the needs of those in our community who are most vulnerable). I believe this is an opportunity to show the entire city that any neighborhood, regardless of its demographics or socio-economic profile, can play an active role in ending homelessness. column51: There are some constituents and Commissioners who believe that the role of an elected official (or at least the role of an ANC Commissioner) is to act as a proxy for the loudest voices in the room. I wholeheartedly reject that vision of governance. After all, if we only listen to our most actively engaged neighbors, our decisions will only represent a limited (and usually narrow) range of interests. Instead, I aim to do the most good I can for the most people that I can. As part of that process, I actively seek consultation from constituents with varying perspectives (even perspectives I disagree with). But ultimately, I think we’re evaluated and elected based on our background, temperament, character and judgment, and that our community expects us to make decisions that are informed by those factors. So, throughout my term, I have listened hard, had many difficult conversations, and done my best to make decisions that were (a) morally and ethically right, (b) representative of the views of my constituents, and (c) in the best interests of the city as a whole. Balancing those three prongs is a challenge for any elected official, and we don’t all prioritize them equally. For me, nothing matters more than simply doing the right thing, even when it’s the hard thing. So if I’m fortunate enough to be re-elected, that’s the approach I’ll continue to follow.
column52: I became Chairperson just five months into my first term, and was tasked with leading a Commission that had, for years, been characterized by chaos, toxicity and bullying. When I was elected to that role, I made a commitment to my colleagues and the public that I would do everything in my power to ensure the Commission operated fairly, transparently, and respectfully. That commitment was put to the test less than a month later, when the District announced its plan to open the Aston. The weeks and months that ensued were difficult and often contentious, but we held more than 10 hours of public meetings, came together to pass a resolution in support of the project, and even held what I believe is the District’s first ever ranked-choice vote (for our appointments to the Aston’s community advisory team). It wasn’t always smooth sailing, but I’m incredibly proud of the work we did on behalf of the community. And I believe that throughout this process, I demonstrated that my commitment to the good governance principles I talk about is real. Because of that, I think I’ve earned the trust of my neighbors — many of whom disagree with me about certain policy issues — because they know that I’ll always be honest with them and I’ll always treat them fairly. With that in mind, I think I’m the right person to represent my SMD because I have been deeply engaged with the District regarding the Aston since Day 1, and I understand how important it is, for both the sake of this community and the people the facility will serve, to make sure the city implements and operates this program effectively. We have an opportunity to do something truly impactful, but the community has a right to expect transparency and meaningful communication. I’ll make sure they get it.

column8: The expense of housing is a core challenge to our neighbors and to anyone who would like to become a neighbor in our vibrant community. For some specifics, sections of U St west of 14th St (including the fire/police station between 16th and 17th) are strong candidates for increasing housing density in order to welcome additional neighbors and bring down the cost of housing for everyone.  There are infill locations along 14th St, Florida, 9th St and elsewhere that are also strong candidates, though significant development has already occurred, which is positive.  On the other hand, we should respect the history of structures such as the True Reformer Building and others that are core to the identity of the community.

column50: Public Safety, we need DC agencies and nonprofits to agree on a cohesive, shared plan of action instead of the reactive efforts we've seen so far  Coordinate with other ANCs to push the DC government to fully implement the CJCC's Gun Violence Reduction Strategic Plan - we would send letters to the Deputy Mayor Appiah and ask for regular updates;  advocate for more funding for business beat officers on the 14th Street NW corridor near the civic plaza to fight back against a rash of thefts in the last few months; continue to host public safety summits with representatives from DC's criminal justice space - DBH, MPD, ONSE, OAG and violence interrupters - and local stakeholders - like the Latin American Youth Center and tenants associations - so residents can directly ask these agencies what the plan is to drive down crime. The first one I held only had government representatives and I realize that was a mistake - we need NGO's and other stakeholders at the table; notify more residents of the CameraConnect program while also passing a resolution to ensure this new program and the Real-Time Crime Center adheres to civil liberties standards; advocate for the passage of truancy reduction legislation - Councilmembers Allen and Parker are currently developing separate bills - because truancy is a factor in juvenile offenses; finally, I will review the forthcoming 2025 John Hopkins report on the effectiveness of violence interrupters programs and see what changes we need to advocate for in those programs - we have VI's serving in our area and anything we can do to make those programs better, we should do.
column51: What's best for our city might not be immediately beneficial to all of my residents. There's a tension there that Councilmembers and ANC's need to respect and acknowledge, even if we can't happily resolve it. The Mayor's now completed goal of 35,000 new houses in the District by 2025 or new priority bus lanes that decrease parking are good for the District as a whole and I support them. When I have residents who oppose them or have issues with them, I try to walk them through the benefits and identify ways to respond to them - is it the look of the home worrying you? If I showed you the design of the home development would that allay your concerns? Is it the design of the bus route that's problematic? - and I've noticed that people respect that I take into consideration their views and respond even if we ultimately disagree.
column52: I meet my residents where they are, rather than expect them to how up at ANC meetings. Every two months I pick a block and knock on the doors of my residents to inform them what I'm doing and ask for their feedback. Every quarter I drop off newsletters on their doors. I've worked hard for my residents and it's led to results through my work with Councilmember Nadeau - more funding for DPW in our area so trash collection is more consistent, MPD responding quickly to issues raised to me by my residents, and public safety events to keep our residents informed. Councilmember Nadeau recognized these accomplishments when she endorsed my re-election and I will continue accomplishing more for my residents.      Being an ANC is hard work but it's rooted me in a community. And I don't take that community or the responsibility they've given me as their advocate for granted.

column51: As an ANC commissioner, making decisions that balance the interests of my constituents and the broader District of Columbia requires a thoughtful and inclusive approach. Here’s how I would navigate this responsibility:    Engage with the Community:    Town Hall Meetings: Regularly host town hall meetings to hear directly from constituents about their concerns and priorities.  Surveys and Polls: Utilize surveys and online polls to gather a wider range of opinions on specific issues.  Office Hours: Offer open office hours where constituents can meet with me one-on-one to discuss their issues.  Stay Informed:    Data-Driven Decisions: Base decisions on data and evidence, using resources from the District to understand the broader impact of issues.  Expert Consultations: Consult with experts and stakeholders on complex issues to gain a deeper understanding of the potential impacts of decisions.  Transparent Communication:    Regular Updates: Provide regular updates through newsletters, social media, and community boards to keep constituents informed about ongoing issues and decisions.  Decision-Making Rationale: Clearly explain the rationale behind decisions, emphasizing how they align with both local and district-wide interests.  Balanced Approach:    Weighing Interests: Carefully weigh the interests of the local community against those of the broader District. Consider how decisions will affect not only immediate constituents but also neighboring areas and the District as a whole.  Equitable Solutions: Strive for solutions that provide equitable benefits across different communities, ensuring that no single group is disproportionately impacted.  Collaboration:    Work with Other Commissioners: Collaborate with fellow ANC commissioners to understand their perspectives and the needs of their constituents, fostering a cohesive approach to district-wide issues.  Partnerships: Partner with local organizations, businesses, and advocacy groups to leverage resources and expertise in addressing community challenges.  Long-Term Vision:    Sustainable Development: Focus on long-term impacts and sustainability when making decisions, ensuring that they contribute positively to the future of the District.  District Goals: Align decisions with the overarching goals and plans of the District of Columbia, such as economic development, public safety, and environmental sustainability.  By actively engaging with the community, staying informed, communicating transparently, balancing interests, collaborating with others, and maintaining a long-term vision, I would make decisions that reflect the best interests of both my constituents and the entire District of Columbia.
column52: I believe I am the right person to serve as an ANC commissioner for my Single-Member District (SMD) because of my unique blend of skills, experiences, and dedication to community service. Here are the key reasons why I am well-suited for this role:    Community Engagement:    Active Listener: I have a proven track record of engaging with diverse groups and listening to their concerns. This skill is crucial for understanding and representing the needs of all constituents.  Inclusive Approach: I am committed to creating inclusive spaces where every voice can be heard, ensuring that my decisions reflect the collective input of the community.  Professional Experience:    Problem-Solving Skills: My background in design and user experience has equipped me with strong problem-solving skills. I excel at identifying issues, analyzing data, and developing practical solutions.  Project Management: I have extensive experience managing complex projects, which involves coordinating with multiple stakeholders, setting clear goals, and delivering results on time.  Community Focus:    Local Knowledge: Having lived and worked in the area for several years, I have a deep understanding of the local issues, challenges, and opportunities that our community faces.  Commitment to Service: I am passionate about giving back to the community and have been actively involved in various local initiatives and volunteer work.  Transparent and Accountable Leadership:    Open Communication: I prioritize transparent communication and regularly update stakeholders on progress and decisions. This approach fosters trust and accountability.  Ethical Standards: I adhere to high ethical standards and am committed to acting in the best interests of the community, free from personal or political bias.  Vision for the Future:    Sustainable Development: I am dedicated to promoting sustainable development that balances economic growth with environmental preservation and social equity.  Innovation and Progress: I bring a forward-thinking perspective and am eager to introduce innovative solutions that enhance the quality of life for all residents.  Collaboration and Teamwork:    Building Partnerships: I have a talent for building strong partnerships with local organizations, businesses, and other stakeholders to achieve common goals.  Working with Peers: My collaborative approach ensures that I can effectively work with other commissioners and community leaders to address district-wide issues.  In summary, my strong commitment to community service, professional expertise, and inclusive approach make me the right person to serve as an ANC commissioner for my SMD. I am dedicated to representing the interests of my constituents and working collaboratively to create a better future for our community.

column50: Making Connecticut Avenue safer for all users is one of the biggest issues especially now that the plan for Concept C was unceremoniously scrapped by the Mayor. After years of study and engagement - to go back to zero has zero vision. My position on this over the past 4 years as an ANC and years before that - is that Connecticut Avenue is unsafe. It needs major changes to make it safer. People don't want bikes and scooters on the sidewalk, they need to go somewhere. Concept C did that. There is only so much space and some need to learn to share.  Continue to advocate to make Connecticut Avenue safe for all users. I live across the street from the Zoo where we welcome visitors from all over the world. They are not coming here thinking they need to have their head on a swivel in the giant marked crosswalk in front of the Zoo because a driver needs to make a U-turn when the peds have the signal. It is unacceptable and embarrassing how dangerous drivers are here and how we seem to be unable to do anything to prevent this behavior.

column50: Right now, the city is slated to build a sobering center in Columbia Heights. As expected, neighbors have come forward in opposition to it. I believe strongly that every neighbor, regardless of their housing status (without a home, homeowners, and renters) deserve an equal voice in our neighborhood's governance. What we are seeing in Columbia Heights (and across the city) is people struggling to make ends meet, find affordable housing, and suffering from addiction. I believe a sobering center is one piece of the puzzle in helping people not just survive, but live well.  As a commissioner, I will continue to advocate for this invaluable resource, proving to all in our community that it is needed and will have an overall positive impact for all residents. I actually think the issue surrounding the sobering center is a microcosm of broader concerns in Columbia Heights, as we fight ever-rising housing costs, gentrification, unemployment, and the friction that comes from it all. But I'm hopeful about my community and how we can come together to make sure all residents are taken care of.
column51: What I have learned in my time as a commissioner is that not every voice is equally represented at ANC meetings, committees, and on surveys. Many times, the people with most power in our community are the loudest voices, drowning out the needs of those not able to engage with the ANC. I think being a good commissioner requires lots of listening, proactive outreach, and being willing to learn. For example, we had two devastating shootings on 10th Street in my SMD in April. In response, I held a meeting at our local park to share out information with neighbors, hear their concerns, and provide potential solutions outside of just "more policing". But what was important in that moment, as a commissioner, was to balance two things: validating people's fears while stemming the tendency to become suspicious of some neighbors (mainly Black and brown folks) in our community. As commissioners, we need to have the spine to respectfully push back on our neighbors, bring them in, and facilitate conversations that can create long-term conditions of safety for all residents; it is our job to make decisions based on the values of the full community, not just a specific subsection, in and outside of our SMDs.      In my day job, I work with DC youth on postsecondary success. I've seen the impact the pandemic, rising housing costs, a poor job market, racism, and crumbling infrastructure have had on our young people. I believe strongly that the decisions I make at an ANC-level can have far-reaching impacts for how we treat our neighbors, especially our young people. I take that responsibility seriously and I let it guide me in my decision-making.
column52: I have spent my time as a commissioner building relationships, hearing from neighbors, and creating opportunities for folks to engage with each other. Three and a half years in, I feel I have a pulse on my SMD's needs, wants, and hopes. I'm excited to continue organizing events, advocating for progressive change, and pushing my fellow commissioners on issues that impact us all. It takes a while to find your footing as a commissioner; I feel like I've finally found it in my second term and I'm excited to keep our work going. I hope my neighbors feel the same way.

column8: There are plenty of spaces in ANC 2G where housing can be constructed to increase density.    First, for ANC 2G04, we have already made great strides this past term. I've worked hand in hand with neighbors in the Naylor Court Historic District to support and approve zoning relief that will bring a 40-unit building to 1322 9th St NW and more units rounding out Naylor Court on the 900 block of N Street NW. My constituents were excited to welcome more neighbors, and I ensured that our developers were diligent in their community engagement process. There are a couple of parking lots surrounding Naylor Court on 10th Street NW and N Street NW. I know there is interest in what could go in these areas, and I know this community is ready to come together in support when the time comes. Let's not forget about the DC Archives! I've already spoken with neighbors, and we're eager to see what the DC Government plans to do with this huge building. We're ready to partner and plan for the future and our future neighbors.    There are plenty of other places to add density. There is a 45-unit building in the works for 1515 9th St NW, which I was proud to support. I'm excited to see movement for 1200 5th Street NW, where we also have a great opportunity to build more housing and affordable units.    I hope we can continue looking at spaces along New York Avenue NW and Rhode Island Avenue NW where we could build more density as well.

column50: Pedestrian and traffic safety in my neighborhood's alleyways.  If elected, I would address the significant concern of pedestrian and traffic safety in my neighborhood's alleyways. Limited authority does not mean we can't build coalitions to deliver for our constituents. By lobbying agencies and bringing them together, I have been able to find effective solutions for our community.  I would continue my work to limit Blagden Alley to through traffic, implement geofencing for rideshare drivers with a pickup zone on 9th Street, and relocate the loading zone on 9th Street NW. Additionally, I would keep lobbying DDOT to daylight all of our alleyways so that pedestrians, cyclists, and motorists can safely navigate without vehicles or illegally parked service trucks obstructing the view. I will also keep lobbying DDOT to make safety improvements along the 9th Street corridor to limit unclear lane shifts, turning, and loading areas. Increasing visibility and limiting the number, speed, and frequency of cars in our historic alleys is critical to achieving these goals and providing more peace, order, and prosperity for our neighbors.
column51: My values are no secret to my constituents and the public. I pride myself on being an effective communicator and listener. While I may not be able to hear from every single constituent, I strive to be always available and accessible to all. I am active in my community, far beyond my Commissioner duties, and this involvement reflects in my judgment and ability to represent my constituents faithfully.    These values easily extend to the entire District because we are all Washingtonians who want the best for one another. Many of my decisions focus on resolutions and agency accountability initiatives that directly impact my constituents' day-to-day lives. I understand that when something affects neighbors in Shaw, it likely impacts others around the District as well. My decision-making is laser-focused on fighting hard to ensure my constituents are well-served by our government and on identifying and changing systems to benefit the entire District.
column52: I am running for reelection because I am incredibly proud of the accomplishments and work I've completed with and for my constituents. We have improved accessibility in our neighborhood with sidewalk replacements, increased the usability of our public spaces by fixing park lighting and fencing, and initiated traffic safety improvements to make Blagden Alley safer for residents and pedestrians. I have demonstrated the grit, creativity, and coalition-building skills necessary to achieve what might seem impossible.    There is still more work to be done to ensure our government serves constituents well, our streets and sidewalks are safer, and we bring more neighbors to our community. I am ready to roll up my sleeves and continue delivering on behalf of Blagden Alley, Naylor Court, and Shepherd Court. My proven track record of turning challenges into tangible improvements makes me the best person to serve as Commissioner. Together, we can keep making our neighborhood a better place for everyone.

column8: Dupont Circle is one of the densest neighborhoods in the District. But the region, and especially DC, faces a housing crisis that has been made worse by the critical shortage of housing, especially in areas served by transit. Dupont Circle is no stranger to rising housing prices and the displacement that has caused. The housing crisis has larger effects than just forcing people to leave neighborhoods they love or contributing to homelessness and poverty. It also forces those looking for housing to move to areas with less transit and less infrastructure for pedestrians and bicyclists. As a result, this contributes to the loss of natural areas and also contributes to rising greenhouse gas emissions, worsening our climate emergency. These crises are of a scale that no one neighborhood or even jurisdiction can solve it alone. We all must contribute. Every neighborhood must do its part to increase the supply of housing, and especially affordable housing. Within Dupont Circle, I support constructing additional housing units across the neighborhood wherever there are opportunities to do so. This can take many forms, including the addition of accessory dwelling units and by constructing new residential buildings. Dupont Circle is one area that must contribute to the creation of new housing especially because it is already a very walkable, bikeable, and transit-rich neighborhood. We have the infrastructure to support more residents in ways that will not contribute to additional driving and additional greenhouse gas emissions. We should especially focus on converting vacant office space to housing. As a commissioner, I have supported additional housing throughout the neighborhood, including on my own block.

column49: I do not regularly travel by car within the District or the region, including ride-hailing and taxis. My commute trip is from Dupont Circle to western Gaithersburg, and I make this trip by walking, riding Metro, and taking a bus. Most of my trips within the District, including to get groceries or other supplies, I make on foot or by bike. I fully support the District's goals to reduce trips by car, and I will work in my role as Commissioner to make it easier for others in my neighborhood and throughout the District to reduce their reliance on cars. I know that for some people, cars will continue to be something that they need for certain trips, but many people currently make trips by car that could be made safe, easy, and comfortable non-auto trips with proper investments in biking, walking, and transit infrastructure. While reducing dependence on cars is something that many cannot imagine, we really have no choice. We all face an unprecedented climate crisis and we will all sink or swim together. We must reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and we cannot wait until 2050 to do it. All of us must pitch in to solve this crisis by driving less. I am reminded of a quote by the former Mayor of Bogota and current President of Colombia, Gustavo Petro: "A developed country is not a place where the poor have cars. It's where the rich use public transportation." We must make this transition in the District and the region as a whole if we are to have any hope of surviving our climate emergency.
column50: Traffic violence. Too many District residents and visitors are being killed or injured by drivers. This is an epidemic that needs to stop.  I will continue to pressure MPD to enforce the law and DDOT to engineer our streets to be safe for all roadway users.
column51: I have made and will continue to make decisions based on what I believe is best for the District and its residents. Because of the severity of the climate emergency, I view all policy decisions through the lens of climate change, and support those that will alleviate the risks or reduce the impacts and I oppose those that will make the situation worse. This includes policies that will reduce car trips, reduce sprawl, improve the tree canopy, and make sure our neighborhoods have all the services residents need within a short walk, bike ride, or transit trip.
column52: I have been a resident of Dupont Circle since 2017. I served on ANC 2B's Transportation & Public Infrastructure Committee and later on the Mobility Committee for 4 years as a resident. I have served as a Commissioner for nearly 2 years. I am also a transportation planner and project manager by profession. I have long been concerned with traffic violence and the climate emergency, and every day, I work professionally to deal with these crises. I will continue to bring this experience and this passion to my role as a Commissioner for ANC 2B. As someone who has worked in local government for nearly 20 years, I understand the planning process and know how to work through channels to get things done. Dupont Circle is already one of DC’s best neighborhoods, and I want to ensure that it stays a great neighborhood. But it must be one that is safe and welcoming for everyone. Keeping Dupont Circle great does not mean keeping it the same, however. All neighborhoods must change and adapt over time. In Dupont Circle, I understand that the lack of housing growth means that longtime residents are being displaced and we are losing the diversity that has long been a hallmark of the neighborhood. And the resistance to changing our streets to make them safer has a cost, too: A cost measured in lives lost or irrevocably changed.

column50: There have been attempts in the past to rezone Columbia Plaza for commercial redevelopment, and the issue will arise again in the Comprehensive Plan review. column51: I am committed to representing all of my constituents equitably and impartially. In my first term as ANC, I grew an email and WhatsApp list to over 100 constituents and will continue to make the outreach to expand public participation. I send a monthly newsletter that includes the matters before the Commission and encourage constituent feedback and participation in our meetings. Our ANC places high value on public participation and welcomes public question and comment on all of our agenda items, a practice I intend to encourage and preserve. I do my best to weigh all input I receive and am careful to approach each issue with consideration to the voices I may not be hearing. Rawlsian analysis grounds my decision-making in its ethical framework to ensure that I act in a way that is both representative of my constituents and in the best interest of all residents of the District.
column52: I am an urban planning student at Georgetown and am passionate about the local political matters that affect our community. I have made strong progress in my first term to expand my outreach and weigh in on important matters in the budget cycle, advocating for our unhoused neighbors, and for improvements to the building code guidelines for two-pipe HVAC systems. There are many other important issues on the horizon, most of all the Comprehensive Plan. I would be honored to serve a second term to continue this important work.

column8: Happily, most of ANC 6E is zoned as Downtown (D), meaning high-density residential construction is heavily encouraged. In all cases in which developers are seeking to move forward with projects in the ANC, I will push to include the maximum allowable density. In SMD 6E06 specifically, for example, the undeveloped lot where Wunder Garten currently sits is owned by a San Francisco-based real estate firm that appears to be waiting for the market to improve to sell. (A developer who previously planned to develop the lot passed away.) I’ll be ready to reach out to negotiate for maximum density and the maximum level of affordable housing units with whoever ends up buying that lot, which is a prime spot one block away from the NoMa-Gallaudet Metro stop. I believe the ANC should take such a preparatory stance for all of our undeveloped or underdeveloped lots. In addition to that, there are a few blocks in the ANC that are zoned as Residential Flat (RF), such as those blocks between New Jersey Avenue and North Capitol Street south almost to H Street. Those RF zones are primarily reserved for row-type dwellings with some limited allowance for conversion to multi-unit dwellings (up to 4 units). In all possible cases, I’ll push to go all the way up to 4 units in those areas. Turning single-family dwellings into multi-unit condos (so-called “missing middle” housing between single-family and multi-unit apartment complexes) could do a lot to address the housing affordability crisis. In the future, looking toward the rewrite of the Comprehensive Plan, I’d push to upzone all RF and RA (Residential Apartment) areas in the ANC to incentivize more high-density development.

column49: I don't own a vehicle, so already the majority of my travel in and around the District is by metro, bicycle, or walking. (I quite enjoy the latter two as daily exercise.) I do, however, use rideshare services on occasion, especially for trips that happen later at night after metro hours. I could cut down on those late-night car trips simply by planning more thoroughly ahead of time in order to make the last metro of the night. Reducing one's carbon footprint takes dedicated attention; it can't be an afterthought.
column50: The biggest issue in my neighborhood is housing availability and affordability. The housing crisis compounds every other socioeconomic problem faced by District residents (and Americans generally). When you’re in a state of housing insecurity or near-insecurity--when 50% of your paycheck is going to housing, for example--every other problem is magnified: inflation bites harder, health emergencies are more damaging, and opportunities to move up diminish. My position is wholly for reducing housing costs in every possible way. The most obvious line of attack is simply to increase supply: build more housing and, wherever possible, more affordable housing. (Please see my website’s “My Priorities” page for the full suite of policies I’m interested in.)  If elected, I would universally push to maximize the height and density of all new residential developments in order to increase the number of affordable units constructed under the District’s inclusionary zoning law. In cases of planned unit developments (PUDs), I’d push for more affordable housing as part of all community benefits agreements. At a higher level, I’d use the “great weight” power of the office of ANC to amplify these concerns by crafting resolutions addressed to all agencies involved with housing, as well as to the Council and the Office of Planning in anticipation of the Comprehensive Plan rewrite next year. Whenever possible, I’d also testify in front of the Council and/or help my fellow ANC members to craft testimony that would effectively and accurately reflect these concerns.
column51: Firstly, I’d make every effort to increase visibility, transparency, and communication in order to be as representative as I can possibly be. In fact, that’s one of my four priorities as listed on my website. What that would look like is a continually updating website, monthly scheduled meetings, and a newsletter. Beyond that, my decision-making process would always be guided by the question: “How will this affect the less privileged, less wealthy, less heard members of the community?” Studies (e.g., “Who Participates in Local Politics?” by Katherine Einstein et al.) show that, by far, the most influential community members in local politics are wealthy, white, older, home-owning, and leisured. I make no bones about wishing to act as a counterweight against that group and their preferences. As for what my process will look like, I’m prepared to lean heavily on my fellow ANC members who will have more experience and know-how than me. My decision-making process would be one of pragmatism, modesty, rigorous empiricism, constant outreach, and continuous learning.
column52: I don't particularly think I'm the "right" person. Everywhere you look in D.C., you'll find plenty of dedicated, impressive people with a sincere drive to help. We're blessed to live in a city like D.C. where intelligent, idealistic people from around the world flock to try to do good work. What I do think, however, is that I'm up to the job and well prepared. A quote that guides me is this, from the author Haruki Murakami: "What we needed were not words and promises but a steady accumulation of small realities." I’ve spent much of my career trying to carve out opportunities for the "accumulation of small realities" in the lives of everyone, especially those facing discrimination, neglect, villainization, and systemic disadvantage. I’m comfortable getting into the nitty-gritty. In the Peace Corps, I partnered with lots of fine people in my community to launch local development projects. Now, as a volunteer with Miriam’s Kitchen, which is an excellent organization taking a comprehensive approach to address the housing crisis, I have my boots on the ground with regard to housing policy, which is my chief concern. Change begins locally - but it takes a comprehensive view. I believe my day job with the federal government provides me with that, too: a sense of how to make things move in a system of overlapping bureaucratic processes, offices, and people.

column8: The Reservoir District (formerly, McMillan Park) is of course one of the most important infill development sites remaining in DC. I'm incredibly excited to see both the added residential and commercial uses. While it's passed most of the critical permitting milestones, the ANC likely will continue to have a role in the development such as through amendments to the PUD (see e.g. https://x.com/ScottRobertsDC/status/1813369561040765222).     There are other infill opportunities within SMD 5E01. Perhaps most excitingly, the Langston Slater properties, a now-abandoned set of former schools, is likely to be developed into mixed-income housing (https://dmped.dc.gov/page/langstonslater).    Beyond that, there are general opportunities for continued densification along the arterial roadways--North Capitol would perhaps most significantly benefit from additional densification and it would be great to see the day where there's a more accessible streetscape between North Capitol and NoMa.    I am so fortunate to have had the opportunity to find a home in Truxton Circle. But if you look at median rent or median home prices in the zip code (20001), both have just about doubled in a decade. That's primarily a function of more people wanting to live here than there are homes currently available. The simple fact is that ensuring Truxton Circle remains accessible to a variety of families of different incomes and backgrounds means supporting building more homes in and across ANC 5E. And that is what I will support, regardless of if I am on the ANC or not.

column51: There's pretty good research at this point showing how local representatives are highly responsive to non-representative negative feedback (Neighborhood Defenders is a great book on this). I have been attending community meetings for years, and have always striven to be a voice for those who cannot attend such meetings--parents with young children, shift workers who can't skip work, renters, and even future neighbors--the folks who might want to live in an area but cannot afford to or do not have the opportunity to due to the high cost of homes. It's not easy, but I think it's as straightforward as the prompt suggests: being an ANC representative means caring about the District as a whole and that can mean taking tough votes. It doesn't mean ignoring the folks who are willing and able to show up, but it does require having an intentionality about remembering that those voices aren't always representative.
column52: Too often I've seen how a single ANC commissioner can be the blocking point for something as simple as a speed bump. I think it's important to have ANC commissioners who have a vision for a District that's designed for the people within it, so that they can get around safely and efficiently, regardless if they are walking, using a wheel chair, biking, or taking the bus.    Similarly, I believe that the District as a whole, my SMD included, is a place of immense opportunity--for jobs, for culture, for recreation, and for meeting new people and generating new ideas. Being a welcoming neighbor--being open to new residents, new buildings, new shops, and new restaurants is key to ensuring that we continue to thrive. ANCs have an immense role to play in facilitating our dynamism or, alternatively, slowing it down, both formally through the votes they take but also through how they organize and echo the feedback from their community.    I hope to be a voice in favor of safe streets, safe communities, and housing abundance, and I think that's both what the District and my SMD would most benefit from.    Beyond that, at its core being an effective ANC commissioner is just being the squeaky wheel for all of the mundane issues that arise in a city--dealing with a garbage bin that the city hasn't delivered yet, or helping figure out who to contact over a water billing issue. I have extensive professional experience navigating bureaucracies so I think I'll be effective in that day-to-day role of being a conduit for more-effective neighborhood services.

column50: I feel the biggest issue in my neighborhood is the high cost of living, and my position on it is: when the cost of living skyrockets while wages are flat, it leads to violent crime, displacement, hunger, health complications, substance addiction, self-harm, and many other destructive cycles.  If elected, I would: do what I'm already doing and focus on tangible actions commissioners can control: guide new small business owners through bureaucracy; aid constituents with unemployment applications; testify to DC Council for more affordable energy sources; urge developers to include family units in their new properties; call out union-busting employers for harm they cause their employees.
column51: We live in a representative democracy, and ANC commissioners do so without any full-time staff or ability to survey constituents on a regular basis. My approach to making informed decisions begins with “meeting people where they’re at” in order to assess public opinion. This means maintaining an interactive website, monitoring email accounts, responding to text messages and phone calls, going door-to-door to have informal conversations, organizing community events, facilitating community input during official ANC meetings, and even perusing social media posts. In addition to public opinion, I find it is important to also stay up-to-date with what trained professionals are saying. That means reading academic journal articles, local journalism, industry white papers, governmental policy pieces, and many other sources of information, published or otherwise. All together, this can be incredibly labor intensive. Fortunately, I have many years of experience practicing these skills and am comfortable doing so both effectively and efficiently.
column52: Adams Morgan residents are not shy about what a community leader needs to bring to the table: a balance of youthful vibrance and mature foresight; impressive accolades and “real world” grit; ambitious goals and pragmatic plans for achieving them. My mix of professional experience, academic training, public service, and willingness to step out of my comfort zone —maybe even getting a little creative when doing so—make me an excellent fit for Adams Morgan. Serving as ANC commissioner means knowing your neighbors and mitigating harm in pursuit of a more prosperous city. Most importantly, it means practicing empathy. I am willing, able, and excited to utilize all the above and continue serving as Commissioner for the Adams Morgan community in ANC 1C.

I am committed to keeping my carbon footprint as minimal as possible. I would add, in case there is a person reading this, that the options presented for Questions 18 and 19 were all good. I would equally fight for all of them, and they are hard to rank. We need more mobility options and safer streets for our seniors and families with children. We need to double and triple down on Vision Zero and center pedestrians in everything we do. As a biker, we need protected bike lanes and I'm tired of sacrificing my safety for drivers who want to take a right turn on red or for individuals to have a parking space. But I also recognize it is a policy failure that encourages individuals to hold onto their cars. We should have busses (with their own protected priority lanes) more frequently. column51: As a representative, being a statesman vs. a politician is top priority. My priority is doing everything for the common good of the people, not special interest. As a Commissioner, I aim to meet people where they are and to be a voice where they are not heard. Ensuring accountability is being met with opportunities to deter systemic oppressive activity that can result in inhumane action from our city government and agencies. I believe we are stronger together, and every voice matters. Whether I disagree or not, no voice will be left behind.
